title: Acute Viral Hepatitis
authors:
Esperance A. K. Schaefer,

Raymond T. Chung, Jules L. Dienstag

items.title: Identifying Causes of Acute Viral Hepatitis
items.content: Acute viral hepatitis predominantly affects the liver and is caused by one of five main viruses: HAV, HBV, HCV, HDV, or HEV. These viruses are primarily RNA viruses except for HBV, which is a DNA virus but replicates like a retrovirus.
keyPoints: Five viral agents cause acute viral hepatitis: HAV, HBV, HCV, HDV, and HEV.
All human hepatitis viruses produce clinically similar acute illnesses ranging from asymptomatic to fulminant infections.
HBV is unique as a DNA virus that replicates like a retrovirus.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis

items.title: Understanding the Spectrum of Acute and Chronic Hepatitis
items.content: While all types of viral hepatitis can cause acute illnesses, HBV, HCV, and HDV are also associated with chronic liver disease. This spectrum ranges from subclinical infections to severe conditions like cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.
keyPoints: HBV, HCV, and HDV can lead to chronic diseases including cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.
Acute viral hepatitis can present as asymptomatic or fulminant infection.
Bloodborne types (HBV, HCV, HDV) have a broader spectrum of disease progression.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis

items.title: Characteristics of Hepatitis A Virus (HAV) Infection
items.content: Hepatitis A virus is a nonenveloped, heat-, acid-, and ether-resistant RNA virus. It replicates in the liver but can be found in bile, stools, and blood during certain phases of infection. Despite nucleotide sequence variation among isolates, all HAV strains are immunologically indistinguishable.
keyPoints: HAV is a nonenveloped 27-nm RNA virus resistant to heat, acid, and ether.
Quasi-enveloped particles circulate in the bloodstream.
Inactivation methods include boiling for 1 min, formaldehyde, chlorine, or ultraviolet irradiation.
All HAV strains belong to one serotype despite up to 20% nucleotide sequence variation.
Incubation period is ~3–4 weeks.
Virus present in liver, bile, stools, and blood during late incubation and acute preicteric/presymptomatic phase.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis of Hepatitis A
items.content: Hepatitis A is diagnosed by detecting anti-HAV IgM during acute illness. Anti-HAV IgG becomes predominant post-convalescence, providing lifelong immunity.
keyPoints: Anti-HAV IgM detected during acute illness when serum aminotransferase activity is elevated and fecal HAV shedding occurs.
IgM anti-HAV persists for several (~3) months, rarely up to 6–12 months.
Post-acute illness, IgG anti-HAV becomes predominant and remains detectable indefinitely.
Presence of serum anti-HAV indicates immunity to reinfection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) Characteristics
items.content: HBV is a compact DNA virus with overlapping genes encoding multiple viral products. It replicates via reverse transcription and has been associated with both acute and chronic hepatitis, as well as hepatocellular carcinoma.
keyPoints: HBV is a 3200-bp circular DNA virus with four overlapping genes: S, C, P, X.
Replicates using reverse transcription of minus-strand DNA from pregenomic RNA.
Entry into hepatocytes mediated by NTCP receptor.
Associated with acute and chronic hepatitis and hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Understanding HBV Particulate Forms and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: Hepatitis B virus (HBV) circulates in three particulate forms: complete virions, 22-nm subviral particles, and 42-nm double-shelled spherical particles. The most abundant are the 22-nm particles, representing excess viral envelope protein, while the 42-nm particles are intact virions.
keyPoints: Three HBV particulate forms: complete virion, 22-nm subviral particles, and 42-nm double-shelled spherical particles.
22-nm particles are most numerous in serum, representing excess viral envelope protein.
42-nm particles represent intact hepatitis B virions and are less common by a factor of 100 to 1,000 compared to 22-nm particles.
HBsAg concentration can reach up to 500 µg/mL; virus particle concentration can be as high as 10 trillion per milliliter.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Genotypic Variations of HBV and Their Clinical Outcomes
items.content: HBV is classified into ten genotypes (A–J) with multiple subtypes, influencing clinical outcomes. Genotype B is associated with less aggressive liver disease progression compared to genotypes C, D, or F.
keyPoints: Ten HBV genotypes (A–J) exist, determined by unique envelope HBsAg gene sequences.
Genotypes A and D are common in the US and Europe; B and C predominate in Asia.
Clinical outcomes vary by genotype: Genotype B is linked to slower liver disease progression and lower hepatocellular carcinoma risk than genotypes C, D, or F.
In a Japanese cohort, 23.4% of patients with genotype A had persistent infection post-acute infection compared to 8.6% with non-A genotypes.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Role and Detection of HBeAg in Hepatitis B Infection
items.content: HBeAg is a soluble nucleocapsid protein that serves as a marker for HBV replication and infectivity. Its presence in serum indicates active viral replication, while its disappearance suggests potential clinical improvement.
keyPoints: HBeAg is produced from the C gene's precore region and secreted into circulation.
HBsAg-positive serum with HBeAg indicates high infectivity (>90% transmission rate from HBsAg-positive mothers to offspring).
Transient appearance of HBeAg early in acute hepatitis B may predict clinical improvement.
Persistence of HBeAg beyond 3 months suggests potential chronic infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 3)

items.title: Transmission Risk Based on Serologic Markers
items.content: The presence of HBeAg in HBsAg-positive serum correlates with higher infectivity and transmission risk, particularly from mother to child.
keyPoints: HBsAg-positive mothers who are HBeAg positive have a >90% chance of transmitting hepatitis B to their offspring.
HBsAg-positive mothers with anti-HBe have a much lower transmission rate (10–15%).
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 3)

items.title: Clinical Implications of HBV Serologic Markers
items.content: The sequence and timing of serologic markers provide insights into the course of hepatitis B infection, from acute to chronic phases.
keyPoints: HBsAg is detectable in serum within 1–12 weeks post-infection, usually between 8 and 12 weeks.
HBsAg precedes clinical symptoms by 2–6 weeks and remains during the symptomatic phase.
In self-limited cases, HBsAg disappears 1–2 months after jaundice onset and rarely persists beyond 6 months.
Anti-HBs becomes detectable after HBsAg disappearance and remains indefinitely.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 3)

items.title: Understanding the 'Window Period' in Hepatitis B Infection
items.content: The window period in hepatitis B infection is a phase where HBsAg has disappeared, but anti-HBs has not yet appeared. During this time, IgM anti-HBc may be the only serologic evidence of current or recent HBV infection.
keyPoints: IgM anti-HBc predominates during the first 6 months after acute infection.
The window period is rarely encountered due to increased sensitivity of immunoassays for HBsAg and anti-HBs.
Isolated anti-HBc does not necessarily indicate active virus replication; it often represents past infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 4)

items.title: Distinguishing Recent from Remote HBV Infections
items.content: The immunoglobulin class of anti-HBc helps distinguish between recent and remote HBV infections. IgM anti-HBc indicates current or recent infection, while IgG anti-HBc suggests past infection.
keyPoints: IgM anti-HBc is predominant during the first 6 months post-infection.
IgG anti-HBc predominates beyond 6 months and in chronic HBV infection.
In ≤1–5% of acute HBV cases, low levels of HBsAg make IgM anti-HBc crucial for diagnosis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 4)

items.title: Implications of Coexisting HBsAg and Anti-HBs in Chronic Hepatitis B
items.content: Coexistence of HBsAg and anti-HBs in chronic hepatitis B patients, especially those who are HBeAg-negative, is associated with a higher risk of advanced fibrosis and cirrhosis.
keyPoints: 4.2% of 6534 Chinese patients with chronic hepatitis B showed coexisting HBsAg and anti-HBs.
This pattern was linked to a higher risk of advanced fibrosis and cirrhosis, likely due to longer disease duration.
Patients with this serologic pattern should be evaluated for advanced disease despite not signaling imminent clearance.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 4)

items.title: Understanding the Replicative Phases of Chronic HBV Infection
items.content: Chronic HBV infection progresses through replicative and nonreplicative phases. The replicative phase is marked by high levels of HBV DNA, maximal infectivity, liver injury, and presence of HBeAg. Over time, approximately 10% per year, patients may transition to the nonreplicative phase, characterized by seroconversion from HBeAg to anti-HBe, reduced liver injury, and integration of HBV DNA into the host genome.
keyPoints: HBV DNA detectable in serum and hepatocyte nuclei during early chronic infection
HBeAg is a qualitative marker; HBV DNA is a quantitative marker of replicative phase
~10% per year transition from replicative to nonreplicative phase
Seroconversion from HBeAg to anti-HBe marks the nonreplicative phase
In nonreplicative phase, circulating HBV DNA levels ≤10³ virions/mL with PCR
Liver injury and infectivity are negligible below this replication threshold
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 5)

items.title: Distinguishing Acute from Chronic Hepatitis B Infection
items.content: Differentiating acute hepatitis B from an exacerbation of chronic HBV infection can be challenging. IgM anti-HBc may reappear during acute exacerbations, making it unreliable as a sole marker for distinguishing between the two. Patient history and follow-up monitoring are crucial in these cases.
keyPoints: IgM anti-HBc can reappear during acute exacerbations of chronic hepatitis B
Reliance on IgM vs. IgG anti-HBc alone may not reliably distinguish acute from chronic infection
Patient history and follow-up monitoring are essential for accurate differentiation
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 5)

items.title: Precore Mutations in Chronic HBV Infection
items.content: A precore mutation, specifically a single base substitution from G to A at nucleotide 1896, results in an HBeAg-negative phenotype by introducing a stop codon that prevents the translation of HBeAg. This mutation is associated with severe liver disease progression and fulminant hepatitis B cases observed in Mediterranean countries.
keyPoints: Precore mutation: G to A substitution at nucleotide 1896
Results in HBeAg-negative phenotype
Associated with rapid progression to cirrhosis or advanced chronic hepatitis B
Fulminant hepatitis B clusters observed in Japan and Israel among patients with this mutation
HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B is prevalent in Mediterranean countries, Europe (~30–40% of cases in the U.S.)
Characterized by lower HBV DNA levels (≤10⁵ IU/mL) and variable aminotransferase activity
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 6)

items.title: Escape Mutants in HBV
items.content: HBsAg escape mutants arise due to a single amino acid substitution from glycine to arginine at position 145, leading to loss of neutralizing activity by anti-HBs. These mutants are observed in individuals with humoral immunologic pressure such as post-vaccination or liver transplant recipients treated with monoclonal antibodies.
keyPoints: Escape mutant: Glycine to Arginine substitution at position 145
Results in loss of neutralizing activity by anti-HBs
Observed in vaccine recipients acquiring HBV and liver transplant recipients on anti-HBs treatment
Raises concerns for vaccination strategies and serologic diagnosis
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 6)

items.title: Extrahepatic Sites of HBV Presence
items.content: HBV antigens and DNA are found in extrahepatic sites like lymph nodes, bone marrow, and spleen. Although not associated with tissue injury at these sites, their presence may explain HBV recurrence post-liver transplantation.
keyPoints: HBV antigens and DNA identified in extrahepatic sites
Sites include lymph nodes, bone marrow, circulating lymphocytes, spleen, pancreas
Presence does not cause tissue injury at these sites
May explain HBV recurrence after liver transplantation
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 6)

items.title: HDV Co-infection and Superinfection Dynamics
items.content: Hepatitis D virus (HDV) can co-infect with Hepatitis B virus (HBV) or superinfect an HBV-infected individual. In superinfections, HDV assumes the HBsAg subtype of the recipient rather than the donor.
keyPoints: HDV requires HBV for replication and expression.
In superinfection, HDV adopts the HBsAg subtype of the recipient.
The duration of HDV infection is limited by the duration of HBV infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 7)

items.title: HDV Genotype and Disease Severity
items.content: Genotypic variation in HDV affects disease severity, with genotype 2 linked to milder disease and genotype 3 associated with severe acute disease.
keyPoints: Genotypes of HDV include eight identified types, predominantly genotype 1.
Genotype 2 is associated with milder disease outcomes.
Genotype 3 is linked to more severe acute disease.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 7)

items.title: HDV Replication and Antigen Detection
items.content: HDV replication suppresses HBV replication, leading to lower levels of HBV. HDV antigen is primarily expressed in hepatocyte nuclei but can be detected in serum during active replication.
keyPoints: HDV replication tends to suppress HBV replication.
HDV antigen is mainly found in hepatocyte nuclei and occasionally in serum.
During acute infection, anti-HDV IgM predominates; high-titer anti-HDV persists in chronic infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 7)

items.title: Understanding the Genetic Structure and Replication of HCV
items.content: Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) has a complex genome that includes an untranslated region with an internal ribosomal entry site (IRES), three structural proteins, and seven nonstructural proteins. The replication process involves several host cellular proteases and viral NS proteins, facilitating efficient assembly and release of the virus.
keyPoints: The HCV genome consists of an IRES adjacent to genes for structural proteins: nucleocapsid core protein (C), E1, and E2; and nonstructural proteins: p7, NS2, NS3, NS4A, NS4B, NS5A, and NS5B.
The 5' untranslated region and core gene are highly conserved among genotypes, while the envelope proteins vary significantly.
NS3 helicase, NS3-4A serine protease, NS5A, and NS5B RNA-dependent RNA polymerase are crucial for virus replication.
HCV replicates at a high rate of $10^{12}$ virions per day with a half-life of 2.7 hours.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 8)

items.title: Host Interactions and Entry Mechanisms in HCV Infection
items.content: HCV entry into hepatocytes involves several host receptors, including CD81 and claudin-1. The virus utilizes a lipoprotein masquerade to evade the immune system, which is facilitated by its assembly and secretion pathway similar to low-density lipoproteins.
keyPoints: HCV enters hepatocytes via CD81 receptor and liver-specific tight junction protein claudin-1.
Additional host receptors include occludin, LDL receptors, glycosaminoglycans, scavenger receptor B1, and epidermal growth factor receptor.
Cyclophilin A binds to NS5A, aiding in viral replication; miR-122 is a liver-specific microRNA involved in HCV replication.
HCV's lipoprotein masquerade limits its visibility to the adaptive immune system.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 8)

items.title: Genotypic Diversity and Clinical Implications of HCV
items.content: There are at least six major genotypes and over 50 subtypes of HCV, with significant sequence divergence. This genetic diversity affects treatment choices and clinical outcomes, such as hepatic steatosis in genotype 3.
keyPoints: HCV has six major genotypes and a minor genotype 7, with >50 subtypes within genotypes.
Genotype differences can influence direct-acting antiviral treatment choices and clinical progression.
Genotypes differ by ≥30% sequence homology; subtypes differ by ~20%; intragenotypic variations are referred to as quasispecies.
The high mutation rate of HCV leads to genotypic diversity, complicating effective humoral immunity.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 8)

items.title: Why Molecular Amplification is Crucial for Detecting HCV RNA
items.content: Molecular amplification techniques, such as PCR, are essential for detecting HCV RNA due to its low concentration in the blood. This method allows for early detection of HCV infection, often before anti-HCV antibodies appear.
keyPoints: HCV RNA is the most sensitive indicator of HCV infection.
Detection requires molecular amplification (e.g., PCR).
Quantitative assays can detect as low as 5 IU/mL.
HCV RNA appears within days post-exposure, before anti-HCV antibodies.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 9)

items.title: How HCV RNA Quantification Standardization is Achieved
items.content: Standardization of HCV RNA quantification across laboratories and assays is achieved by reporting the viral load in international units per milliliter, ensuring consistency and comparability.
keyPoints: HCV RNA reported as international units (IUs) per milliliter.
Quantitative assays have a broad dynamic range.
Sensitivity of detection can be as low as 5 IU/mL.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 9)

items.title: Role of Immune Response in Hepatitis B Pathogenesis
items.content: The clinical manifestations and outcomes of acute liver injury due to hepatitis B are primarily determined by the host's immunologic responses rather than direct cytopathic effects of HBV.
keyPoints: HBV is not directly cytopathic; evidence shows normal liver histology in inactive carriers.
>90% of HBV DNA disappears before maximal T-cell infiltration, suggesting innate immune system involvement.
Robust CD8+ T-cell response is crucial for viral clearance and liver injury.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Innate Immune System's Role in Early HBV Response
items.content: Components of the innate immune system, including NK cells, play a significant role in the early immune response to HBV infection, independent of direct cytopathic mechanisms.
keyPoints: NK cell cytotoxicity and inflammatory cytokines contribute to early HBV elimination.
Innate response involves removal of HBV replicative intermediates from hepatocytes.
Immunologic perturbations are often restored after successful antiviral therapy.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Debate on Viral vs. Host Factors in HBV Pathogenesis
items.content: There is ongoing debate regarding the relative importance of viral and host factors in determining the pathogenicity and outcomes of HBV-associated liver injury.
keyPoints: Precore genetic mutants of HBV are linked to severe outcomes, indicating potential viral pathogenicity.
Concomitant HDV and HBV infections result in more severe liver injury than HBV alone.
Post-transplantation rapidly progressive liver injury suggests possible direct cytopathic effects under immunosuppression.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Mechanism of T-cell Tolerance in Neonatal HBV Infection
items.content: In utero exposure to HBeAg induces T-cell tolerance, explaining the lack of immunologic clearance and chronic infection in neonates.
keyPoints: HBeAg-expressing transgenic mice studies show in utero exposure leads to T-cell tolerance.
Tolerance to nucleocapsid proteins results in lifelong HBV infection when acquired early in life.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: Differences Between Neonatal and Adult-Acquired HBV Infection
items.content: Neonatal HBV infection leads to high immunologic tolerance and chronic infection, while adult-acquired infection typically results in acute illness with low chronicity.
keyPoints: Neonatal acquisition: High tolerance, no acute hepatitis, almost always chronic infection.
Adult acquisition: Robust immune response, acute hepatitis-like illness, low risk of chronicity and hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: Phases of Chronic HBV Infection
items.content: Chronic HBV infection is categorized into immune tolerant, immune active, and inactive phases, with nuances in early-life infections.
keyPoints: Immune tolerant phase: High replication, low necroinflammatory activity in neonatal cases.
Immune active phase: Liver injury and fibrosis emerge later, with substantial tolerance remaining.
Dynamic equilibrium between tolerance and intolerance influences clinical outcomes.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: Role of T cells in Hepatitis C Pathogenesis
items.content: In hepatitis C, both CD4+ helper T cells and CD8+ cytotoxic T cells are crucial for immune response. Virus-activated CD4+ T cells stimulate CD8+ T cells via cytokines, which is more effective in those who recover from HCV infection compared to those with chronic infection.
keyPoints: CD4+ helper T cells stimulate CD8+ cytotoxic T cells
Higher robustness of immune responses correlates with recovery from HCV
Chronic infection linked to CD4+ proliferative defects and mutations in viral epitopes
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.title: Genetic Factors Influencing Hepatitis C Clearance
items.content: The IL28B gene polymorphisms, particularly the CC haplotype, are linked to self-limited hepatitis C due to their role in innate immune defense. Non-CC variants and a chromosome 19q13.13 frameshift variant upstream of IL28B contribute to impaired HCV clearance.
keyPoints: IL28B CC haplotype associated with better HCV clearance
Non-CC IL28B polymorphisms linked to persistent infection
$\Delta$G polymorphism creates ORF in IFN-$ \lambda $4, impairing clearance
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.title: Hepatitis A and E Pathogenesis via T-cell Response
items.content: In hepatitis A and E, liver injury is primarily driven by cytolytic CD8+ T-cell responses. In HAV infection, nonspecific CD8+ T-cell activation against other viruses correlates with liver damage.
keyPoints: Cytolytic CD8+ T-cell response causes liver cell injury in hepatitis A and E
Nonspecific CD8+ T-cell activation linked to liver injury in acute HAV
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.title: Extrahepatic Manifestations of Acute Hepatitis B: Immune Complex-Mediated Disorders
items.content: Acute hepatitis B can lead to immune complex-mediated extrahepatic manifestations, such as serum sickness-like syndrome due to HBsAg-anti-HBs complexes. Other rare complications include polyarteritis nodosa and glomerulonephritis.
keyPoints: Immune complex-mediated tissue damage is a pathogenic mechanism in acute hepatitis B.
Serum sickness-like syndrome involves deposition of HBsAg-anti-HBs immune complexes in vessel walls, activating the complement system and reducing serum complement levels.
Rare extrahepatic manifestations include polyarteritis nodosa, glomerulonephritis with nephrotic syndrome, and cryoglobulinemia.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EXTRAHEPATIC MANIFESTATIONS | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Extrahepatic Manifestations of Hepatitis E: Rare Complications
items.content: Hepatitis E can cause rare neurologic, renal, pancreatic, and hematologic complications due to immunologic mechanisms or direct viral infection.
keyPoints: Rare extrahepatic manifestations in hepatitis E include Guillain-Barré syndrome, renal, pancreatic, and hematologic complications.
These are postulated to result from both immunologic mechanisms and/or direct extrahepatic-site infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EXTRAHEPATIC MANIFESTATIONS | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Histopathology of Acute Viral Hepatitis: When Liver Biopsy is Indicated
items.content: Liver biopsy is rarely needed for diagnosing acute viral hepatitis, as clinical features and serologic tests are sufficient. However, in severe cases like fulminant hepatitis, a transjugular route may be used.
keyPoints: Typical morphologic lesions include panlobular infiltration with mononuclear cells, hepatic cell necrosis, and hyperplasia of Kupffer cells.
Liver biopsy is not routinely performed for acute hepatitis due to sufficient clinical and serologic diagnosis.
In severe cases like fulminant hepatitis, a transjugular route biopsy can be used despite coagulopathy.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EXTRAHEPATIC MANIFESTATIONS | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Transmission and Epidemiology of Hepatitis A
items.content: Hepatitis A virus (HAV) is primarily transmitted via the fecal-oral route, with significant person-to-person spread in settings of poor hygiene and overcrowding. Outbreaks have been linked to contaminated food and water sources, including imported produce and shellfish. The incidence has declined in developed countries due to improved sanitation but remains a concern among high-risk groups.
keyPoints: HAV transmission is almost exclusively fecal-oral.
Outbreaks traced to contaminated food/water (e.g., frozen raspberries, green onions, scallops).
Epidemic waves in temperate zones every 5–20 years; decline in developed countries due to sanitation improvements.
No HAV carrier state post-infection; virus perpetuation through subclinical infections and environmental reservoirs.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Changing Prevalence of Anti-HAV in the U.S.
items.content: The prevalence of anti-HAV, indicating past HAV infection, has decreased over time in the general population but increased among certain age groups. This reflects changes in exposure patterns and vaccination practices, with a notable shift from children to adults as the primary group affected by new infections.
keyPoints: Anti-HAV prevalence was ~40% in U.S. urban populations during the 1970s.
Decline in anti-HAV prevalence due to reduced childhood exposure; >70% reduction in HAV incidence post-vaccination programs.
NHANES data: Anti-HAV prevalence dropped from 29.5% (1999-2006) to 24.2% (≥20 years, 2007-2012).
Increase in anti-HAV among U.S.-born persons aged 2–29 years (30% in 2007-2010 to 40% in 2011-2016).
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Risk Factors and Outbreaks of Hepatitis A
items.content: Certain populations, including those with specific lifestyle factors or living conditions, are at higher risk for HAV infection. Recent outbreaks have highlighted the vulnerability of homeless individuals and injection drug users to person-to-person transmission in contaminated environments.
keyPoints: High-risk groups: childcare centers, neonatal units, MSM, injection drug users, unvaccinated contacts of adopted children from endemic areas.
Outbreaks among homeless and injection drug users; significant outbreak in 2016-2020 across 32 states.
31,950 cases reported by March 2020 with 19,548 hospitalizations (61%) and 322 deaths (1% of cases).
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Older Population at Higher Risk for Severe Hepatitis A Outbreaks
items.content: Hepatitis A outbreaks have shown increased severity, hospitalization rates, and deaths in older populations. This is attributed to their age group being born before the introduction of universal childhood hepatitis A vaccination, leading to higher clinical severity compared to children.
keyPoints: Outbreaks reported across 37 states with severe outcomes.
Mean age of affected population ranged from 36 to 42 years; median age at death was 55.
Older populations were born before universal hepatitis A vaccination, increasing their risk.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Modes of Hepatitis B Transmission Beyond Percutaneous Inoculation
items.content: Hepatitis B transmission is not limited to percutaneous inoculation, with significant nonpercutaneous routes including intimate contact and perinatal transmission. Oral ingestion is a potential but inefficient route.
keyPoints: HBsAg found in almost every body fluid of infected persons; semen and saliva are infectious.
Intimate (especially sexual) contact and perinatal transmission are significant nonpercutaneous routes.
Oral ingestion documented as an inefficient exposure route.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: High Global Prevalence of Chronic HBV Infection
items.content: The global prevalence of chronic HBV infection is estimated at 3.5%, with higher rates in East Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and tropical countries. High-risk groups include those with certain medical conditions, injection drug users, and others.
keyPoints: Over 290 million persons globally have chronic HBV infection.
Global prevalence of HBsAg is estimated at 3.5%, up to 5–10% in East Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and tropical countries.
High-risk groups include individuals with specific medical conditions, injection drug users, and others.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Geographic Variation in HBV Epidemiology and Impact of Immigration
items.content: The epidemiologic patterns of hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection vary geographically due to differences in prevalence, modes of transmission, and human behavior. In East Asia and Africa, maternal-neonatal spread is predominant, whereas in North America and western Europe, it primarily affects adolescents and young adults through sexual contact and percutaneous exposures. Immigration from high-prevalence regions has increased HBV prevalence in low-prevalence areas like the United States.
keyPoints: In East Asia and Africa, HBV is perpetuated by maternal-neonatal spread.
In North America and western Europe, HBV affects adolescents and young adults through sexual contact and percutaneous exposures.
NHANES data (2007-2012) showed a 0.3% overall prevalence of HBV in the U.S., with Asian persons having a tenfold higher prevalence at 3.1%, representing 50% of the national disease burden.
The incidence of newly reported HBV infections fell by >80% during the 1990s and remained low into the 21st century, with 0.6 cases per 100,000 population in 2021.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.title: Universal Screening for Hepatitis B: Rationale and Impact
items.content: The CDC recommended universal screening for hepatitis B in all adults aged ≥18 years starting in 2023. This shift from high-risk group screening to universal screening was due to the ineffectiveness of risk-group screening in reducing HBV prevalence and the discovery that most acute cases occur outside these groups. Universal screening is cost-effective, given the effectiveness of current antiviral therapies in reducing morbidity and mortality associated with chronic hepatitis B.
keyPoints: Universal screening for hepatitis B was recommended by the CDC in 2023 for all adults aged ≥18 years.
Risk-group screening had not effectively reduced HBV prevalence; most acute cases occur outside high-risk groups.
Current antiviral therapy is effective in reducing clinical progression and consequences of HBV infection, making universal screening cost-effective.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)
items.title: Identifying High-Risk Groups for HBV Screening
items.content: The passage outlines specific populations at high risk for HBV infection, emphasizing the importance of targeted screening and vaccination strategies. These groups include individuals from regions with high or intermediate HBV prevalence, household contacts of infected persons, injection drug users, and healthcare workers exposed to blood.
keyPoints: High-risk groups: ≥8% prevalence (e.g., immigrants from endemic areas), ≥2% prevalence
Specific populations: Babies born to HBsAg-positive mothers, men who have sex with men, incarcerated persons
Universal screening recommended for all adults >18 years as of 2023
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)
items.title: Understanding HDV Infection Dynamics
items.content: HDV infection is primarily confined to individuals frequently exposed to blood, such as injection drug users and hemophiliacs. The prevalence of HDV among HBsAg-positive persons varies significantly by risk group, with higher rates observed in injection drug users and hemophiliacs.
keyPoints: Prevalence of HDV: 0.02% (NHANES 1999–2012), 0.11% (NHANES 2011–2016)
High prevalence among HBsAg-positive injection drug users: 11-36%
HDV genotypes distribution: Genotype 1 worldwide, others region-specific

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)
items.title: Impact of Public Health Measures on HDV Prevalence
items.content: Public health interventions, such as mass hepatitis B vaccination, have contributed to the decline in HDV infection rates. However, the global prevalence remains significant due to sustained reservoirs from past infections and recent immigration.
keyPoints: Reduction in HDV prevalence: 1.5%/year during the 1990s in Italy
Current global prevalence of HDV: Estimated at 12 million people
Sustained reservoirs due to survivors from 1970-1980 and recent immigrants
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.title: Impact of Blood Donor Screening on Transfusion-Associated Hepatitis
items.content: The introduction of surrogate screening tests and more sensitive immunoassays, followed by PCR testing, significantly reduced the risk of transfusion-associated hepatitis C to almost imperceptible levels.
keyPoints: Risk of transfusion-associated hepatitis reduced to <5% in the 1980s due to anti-HIV donor screening.
Further reduction with surrogate tests (ALT and anti-HBc) for non-A, non-B hepatitis.
PCR testing for HCV RNA brought risk down to between 1 in 2.3 million and 1 in 4.7 million transfusions.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.title: Prevalence and Transmission of HCV
items.content: Hepatitis C is transmitted through transfusions, percutaneous routes like injection drug use, and occupational exposure. The prevalence varies significantly across populations and regions.
keyPoints: 90% serologic evidence for HCV in patients with transfusion-associated hepatitis before 1992.
60-70% of sporadic 'non-A, non-B' hepatitis cases lack identifiable risk factors.
1.6% prevalence in the general US population (NHANES 1999-2002), translating to 4.1 million persons.
Egypt's high prevalence (>20%) due to historical medical practices.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.title: Epidemiological Trends and Control of HCV in the US
items.content: Despite an 80% reduction in new HCV infections during the 1990s, prevalence remains high due to aging cohorts. Successful control measures include screening and treatment programs.
keyPoints: 80% reduction in reported HCV cases in the 1990s.
Prevalence sustained by aging cohort infected in the 1960s-1970s.
Increase in hepatitis C-related mortality since 1995, mainly in ages 45-65.
Egypt's near elimination of HCV through a national screening and treatment program.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology of HCV in Different U.S. Populations
items.content: The prevalence of current HCV infection has decreased over time among the general domiciled and noninstitutionalized civilian population, but remains significantly higher among incarcerated and homeless individuals.
keyPoints: Prevalence of HCV RNA reactivity fell from 1.3% (1999-2002) to 0.9% (2013-2016) in the general U.S. population.
Estimated prevalence among incarcerated and homeless persons is 10.7%.
NHANES datasets account for only domiciled, noninstitutionalized civilian populations.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.title: Shift in Mortality Trends: HCV vs. HIV
items.content: Age-adjusted mortality from HCV surpassed that of HIV infection post-1999, largely affecting the baby boomer cohort.
keyPoints: HCV-associated age-adjusted mortality surpassed HIV after 2007.
>70% of HCV-related deaths occurred in those born between 1945 and 1965.
By 2012, HCV mortality exceeded the total number of HIV-infected individuals.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.title: Expansion of Hepatitis C Screening Guidelines
items.content: Due to the high efficacy and cost-effectiveness of DAAs, screening recommendations have expanded to include all adults aged 18–79 and pregnant women.
keyPoints: DAAs have 95-99% efficacy and are well-tolerated.
Screening now recommended for all adolescents and adults aged 18–79.
Expanded screening includes pregnant women due to increased HCV infections in child-bearing age.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.title: Understanding the Transmission Dynamics of HCV
items.content: Hepatitis C virus (HCV) transmission is primarily inefficient through sexual and perinatal routes, except in specific high-risk groups. Sexual transmission occurs mainly among individuals with multiple partners or sexually transmitted diseases, while perinatal transmission risk exceeds 10% for babies born to HIV-HCV coinfected mothers.
keyPoints: Sexual transmission of HCV is inefficient (~5%) and rare between monogamous partners (1%).
Perinatal transmission risk >10% in babies from HIV-HCV coinfected mothers.
High-risk sexual subgroups include persons with multiple partners or sexually transmitted diseases.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.title: HCV Transmission in Healthcare Settings and Household Contacts
items.content: Healthcare workers face a higher risk of HCV infection through needle punctures (~3%) compared to the general population. However, household contact transmission is rare.
keyPoints: Healthcare workers have an increased risk of HCV from accidental needle punctures (~3%).
Household contact transmission of HCV is uncommon.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.title: Epidemiological Trends and Screening Recommendations
items.content: The incidence of new HCV infections has risen since 2002, driven by opioid use among young injection drug users. This trend has led to increased prevalence in the U.S., prompting recommendations for screening pregnant women.
keyPoints: New HCV infection rates increased from 0.3/100,000 (2009) to 1.2/100,000 (2018).
Opioid use among young injection drug users aged 20–39 years has significantly contributed to the rise in cases.
Prevalence of current HCV infection rose from 0.65% (2010-2014) to 0.9% (2013-2016), with an estimated prevalence of 0.93% when excluding certain populations.
Screening for HCV is recommended for pregnant women due to increased prevalence.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)
items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Seroprevalence of HEV in Nonendemic Areas
items.content: Hepatitis E virus (HEV) is primarily endemic to regions like India, Asia, Africa, the Middle East, and Central America. In nonendemic areas such as the United States, acute hepatitis E is rare, but subclinical infections are more common due to genotypes 3 and 4. The NHANES III survey indicated a high prevalence of anti-HEV (21%), which decreased significantly in subsequent surveys (6% in 2009–2010) likely due to improved assay specificity.
keyPoints: Acute HEV infections are estimated at 20 million annually worldwide, with 3.3 million symptomatic cases.
In nonendemic areas like the U.S., acute hepatitis E is rare; NHANES III showed a 21% anti-HEV prevalence.
NHANES 2009–2010 reported a reduced anti-HEV prevalence to 6%, reflecting assay improvements and rarity of clinical cases.
Seroprevalence in the U.S. (2009–2016) was 6.1%, with higher rates in older age, non-Hispanic Asians, and those born outside the U.S.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)
items.title: Zoonotic Transmission of HEV in Nonendemic Areas
items.content: In nonendemic regions, HEV transmission is often zoonotic, primarily from swine. This accounts for the subclinical infections seen with genotypes 3 and 4. Globally, about 60% of swine show evidence of prior HEV infection, and 13% have active infections. In Western Europe, ingestion of undercooked pork products has led to high seroprevalence rates.
keyPoints: HEV genotypes 3 and 4 are linked to zoonotic transmission from animals like swine, deer, camels, and rabbits.
Approximately 60% of swine globally have evidence of prior HEV infection; 13% have active infections.
In Western Europe, consumption of undercooked pork is associated with high HEV seroprevalence (e.g., Germany: ~300,000 cases/year, 17% anti-HEV prevalence in adults).
France reports a 22% anti-HEV prevalence among healthy blood donors.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)

items.title: Incubation Periods and Prodromal Symptoms
items.content: The incubation periods for different types of viral hepatitis vary, with hepatitis A ranging from 15 to 45 days, B and D from 30 to 180 days, C from 15 to 160 days, and E from 14 to 60 days. Prodromal symptoms include systemic signs like anorexia, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, and myalgias, often preceding jaundice.
keyPoints: Hepatitis A incubation: 15-45 days (mean 4 weeks)
Hepatitis B/D incubation: 30-180 days (mean 8–12 weeks)
Hepatitis C incubation: 15-160 days (mean 7 weeks)
Hepatitis E incubation: 14-60 days (mean 5–6 weeks)
Prodromal symptoms include anorexia, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, malaise, arthralgias, myalgias, headache, photophobia
Low-grade fever more common in hepatitis A and E
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Jaundice Onset and Clinical Course
items.content: Jaundice onset varies by virus, being more common in acute hepatitis B than C. The prodromal symptoms usually diminish with jaundice onset, but mild weight loss may continue. Liver enlargement and tenderness are typical, and splenomegaly occurs in 10-20% of cases.
keyPoints: Jaundice more common in acute hepatitis B than C
Prodromal symptoms diminish with jaundice onset
Mild weight loss (2.5–5 kg) may continue during icteric phase
Liver enlargement and tenderness typical
Splenomegaly present in 10-20% of patients
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Recovery Phases and HDV Coinfection/Superinfection
items.content: Complete recovery from hepatitis A and E occurs within 1-2 months, while hepatitis B and C take 3-4 months. HDV coinfection with HBV can be mild to fulminant, whereas superinfection often leads to severe clinical deterioration.
keyPoints: Recovery: 1-2 months for hepatitis A/E, 3-4 months for B/C
HDV coinfection with HBV ranges from mild to fulminant
HDV superinfection in chronic HBV is severe and persistent
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Serum Aminotransferase Levels in Acute Viral Hepatitis
items.content: Serum aminotransferases, AST and ALT, increase during the prodromal phase of acute viral hepatitis. These levels do not correlate well with liver cell damage but peak at clinical jaundice onset.
keyPoints: AST and ALT levels rise before bilirubin in acute viral hepatitis.
Peak enzyme levels range from ~400 to >4000 IU/L, typically reached when the patient becomes icteric.
Elevated aminotransferases are diagnostic of anicteric hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Significance of Serum Bilirubin Levels in Acute Viral Hepatitis
items.content: Jaundice becomes visible when serum bilirubin exceeds 43 μmol/L. High levels (>340 μmol/L) late in the disease course suggest severe hepatitis, but must be interpreted with caution in patients with hemolytic anemia.
keyPoints: Jaundice is visible at >43 μmol/L (2.5 mg/dL) serum bilirubin.
Typical bilirubin levels during jaundice range from 85 to 340 μmol/L (5–20 mg/dL).
Bilirubin >340 μmol/L (20 mg/dL) late in hepatitis indicates severe disease; however, levels >513 μmol/L (30 mg/dL) can occur with hemolytic anemia without poor prognosis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Prothrombin Time and Its Prognostic Value in Acute Viral Hepatitis
items.content: Prolonged prothrombin time reflects severe hepatic synthetic defects, extensive hepatocellular necrosis, and indicates a worse prognosis. It may occur even with mild increases in bilirubin and aminotransferase levels.
keyPoints: Prothrombin time is crucial for assessing liver function in acute viral hepatitis.
A prolonged prothrombin time suggests severe hepatic synthetic defects and worse prognosis.
Prolonged PT can occur with mild increases in bilirubin and aminotransferase levels.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Role of HBeAg in Hepatitis B Infectivity
items.content: HBeAg is a serologic marker indicating relative infectivity in hepatitis B, particularly useful during early acute infection. In chronic infections, it helps assess the level of viral replication.
keyPoints: HBeAg indicates relative infectivity.
Present during early acute hepatitis B.
Primarily used for assessing chronic infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.title: Distinguishing Acute from Chronic HBV Infection
items.content: IgM anti-HBc testing helps differentiate acute or recent hepatitis B infections from chronic ones. A false-positive result can occur in patients with high-titer rheumatoid factor.
keyPoints: IgM anti-HBc distinguishes acute/recent from chronic HBV infection.
False positives possible with high-titer rheumatoid factor.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.title: Interpreting Anti-HBs in Chronic Hepatitis B
items.content: Anti-HBs is rarely present with HBsAg during acute hepatitis B but can be found in up to 10% of chronic HBV cases. Its presence correlates with higher aminotransferase levels and advanced liver disease, especially in HBeAg-negative patients.
keyPoints: Anti-HBs rare with HBsAg in acute infection.
Up to 10% prevalence in chronic HBV.
Associated with higher aminotransferase levels and advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.title: Differentiating Acute and Chronic HCV Infections
items.content: In high-risk groups, such as people who inject drugs (PWID), a positive anti-HCV with detectable HCV RNA may indicate acute reinfection rather than chronic infection. Differentiation relies on prior laboratory tests and clinical history.
keyPoints: Anti-HCV is persistent but does not prevent reinfection.
HCV RNA assays are the gold standard for diagnosing hepatitis C.
HCV RNA can be detected before aminotransferase elevation or anti-HCV appearance in acute cases.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.title: Interpreting HCV RNA Levels
items.content: HCV RNA levels, detected via quantitative PCR with a sensitivity of 10 to 25 IU/mL and a dynamic range of 10–10⁷ IU/mL, are not reliable markers for disease severity but help predict responsiveness to antiviral therapy.
keyPoints: Quantitative PCR assays measure viral load with high sensitivity (10-25 IU/mL).
HCV RNA levels aid in predicting treatment response, though not disease severity.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.title: Identifying HDV Infection
items.content: HDV infection can be identified by anti-HDV seroconversion or intrahepatic antigen detection. The presence of IgM anti-HBc indicates recent HBV infection, aiding in distinguishing between acute and chronic HBV/HDV infections.
keyPoints: Anti-HDV (IgM/IgG) seroconversion confirms HDV infection.
IgM anti-HBc is a reliable indicator of recent HBV infection.
In simultaneous acute HBV and HDV, IgM anti-HBc will be detectable.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.title: Serologic Testing for Acute Viral Hepatitis
items.content: In evaluating acute viral hepatitis, five serologic tests are essential: HBsAg, IgM anti-HAV, IgM anti-HBc, anti-HCV, and HCV RNA. These tests help differentiate between types of hepatitis infections.
keyPoints: HBsAg with or without IgM anti-HBc indicates HBV infection.
Presence of IgM anti-HBc suggests acute HBV; absence indicates chronic HBV.
IgM anti-HAV confirms acute hepatitis A.
Anti-HCV presence supports acute hepatitis C diagnosis; if undetectable, test for HCV RNA.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.title: Diagnosing Simultaneous Hepatitis Infections
items.content: Simultaneous infections can be identified by specific serologic markers. For example, IgM anti-HAV with HBsAg indicates concurrent HAV and HBV infections.
keyPoints: IgM anti-HAV + HBsAg = simultaneous HAV and HBV infection.
IgM anti-HBc (with or without HBsAg) + IgM anti-HAV = acute hepatitis A and B.
IgM anti-HAV with undetectable IgM anti-HBc suggests acute hepatitis A on chronic HBV.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.title: Approach to Negative Serologic Tests in Acute Hepatitis
items.content: When standard serologic tests for hepatitis A, B, and C are negative, consider testing for hepatitis E or other causes. Negative HBsAg excludes hepatitis D.
keyPoints: If all serologic assays (A-E) are negative, test for hepatitis E if risk factors are present.
Negative serologic markers for hepatitis B exclude hepatitis D infection.
In high-risk patients with acute hepatitis B, consider testing for anti-HDV and/or HDV RNA.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.title: Prognosis of Acute Viral Hepatitis: Key Factors and Outcomes
items.content: The prognosis for acute viral hepatitis varies significantly based on the type of virus, patient demographics, clinical features, and underlying health conditions. Most previously healthy individuals recover fully from hepatitis A and B, with a 95–99% favorable outcome in adults for hepatitis B. However, severe cases may arise due to factors such as advanced age, serious medical disorders, or specific clinical presentations like ascites, peripheral edema, hepatic encephalopathy symptoms, prolonged PT, low serum albumin, hypoglycemia, and high bilirubin levels.
keyPoints: 95–99% of previously healthy adults recover completely from acute hepatitis B
Case-fatality rate for hepatitis A and B is ~0.1%, increasing with age and underlying disorders
Hospitalization fatality rate for severe acute hepatitis B is 1%
Hepatitis C has a rare fatality rate during the acute phase, but symptomatic cases are more likely to contain the virus
Waterborne hepatitis E case-fatality rates: 1–2% generally; up to 10–20% in pregnant women
Acute-on-chronic liver disease increases risk of fulminant hepatitis E
Coinfection with HBV and HDV does not necessarily increase mortality compared to HBV alone, but simultaneous infection among drug users has a ~5% fatality rate
HDV superinfection in chronic HBV patients significantly raises the risk of fulminant hepatitis and death
In isolated populations with high HBV carrier rates ('Lábrea fever'), severe HDV superinfection mortality can exceed 20%
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROGNOSIS

items.title: Clinical Indicators for Hospital Admission in Acute Viral Hepatitis
items.content: Certain clinical and laboratory features indicate a more complicated course of acute viral hepatitis, necessitating prompt hospital admission. These include advanced age, serious underlying medical conditions, ascites, peripheral edema, symptoms of hepatic encephalopathy, prolonged prothrombin time (PT), low serum albumin levels, hypoglycemia, and very high bilirubin values.
keyPoints: Advanced age and serious underlying disorders suggest a more severe hepatitis course
Clinical features like ascites, peripheral edema, and hepatic encephalopathy symptoms indicate poor prognosis
Laboratory indicators: prolonged PT, low serum albumin, hypoglycemia, high bilirubin levels
Patients with these features should be promptly hospitalized
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROGNOSIS

items.title: Understanding Relapsing Hepatitis A
items.content: A small proportion of hepatitis A patients experience relapses weeks to months after recovery. These are characterized by symptom recurrence, elevated aminotransferase levels, occasional jaundice, and fecal HAV excretion.
keyPoints: Relapsing hepatitis A occurs in a small subset of patients post-recovery.
Symptoms include recurrence of initial symptoms, elevated liver enzymes, occasional jaundice, and fecal HAV presence.
Despite these complications, hepatitis A remains self-limited without progression to chronic liver disease.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.title: Serum Sickness-like Syndrome in Acute Hepatitis B
items.content: During the prodromal phase of acute hepatitis B, a serum sickness-like syndrome may develop in 5–10% of patients. This includes arthralgia or arthritis, rash, angioedema, and rarely hematuria and proteinuria.
keyPoints: Serum sickness-like syndrome occurs before jaundice onset in acute hepatitis B.
Affects 5-10% of patients with symptoms like arthralgia/arthritis, rash, and angioedema.
Diagnosis involves elevated serum aminotransferase levels and detection of HBsAg.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.title: Fulminant Hepatitis: A Rare but Severe Complication
items.content: Fulminant hepatitis is a rare complication primarily seen in hepatitis B, D, and E. It presents with encephalopathy, rapidly rising bilirubin, prolonged PT, and can lead to high mortality without liver transplantation.
keyPoints: Fulminant hepatitis occurs mainly in hepatitis B (over 50% of cases), D, and E.
Characterized by encephalopathy, rapid bilirubin increase, prolonged PT, and potential for coma.
Mortality exceeds 80% without liver transplantation; rare in hepatitis C but more common in pregnant women with hepatitis E.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.title: Progression from Acute to Chronic Hepatitis B
items.content: Acute hepatitis B can progress to chronic hepatitis in certain patients, particularly those with neonatal infection or immunosuppression. Key indicators include persistent clinical symptoms, specific histological findings on liver biopsy, and prolonged presence of HBeAg or HBsAg.
keyPoints: Lack of resolution of anorexia, weight loss, fatigue, and hepatomegaly suggests chronic progression.
Bridging/interface or multilobular hepatic necrosis on biopsy indicates severe acute hepatitis with potential for chronicity.
Persistence of HBeAg >3 months or HBsAg >6 months after acute illness is a marker for chronic hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Hepatitis D on Chronic Hepatitis B
items.content: Hepatitis D superinfection can exacerbate chronic hepatitis B, leading to severe liver disease and increasing the risk of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma. It can also cause fulminant hepatitis in some cases.
keyPoints: Annual rate of cirrhosis in chronic hepatitis D is 4%.
Hepatitis D superinfection can transform mild chronic hepatitis B into severe disease.
Annual risk of hepatocellular carcinoma in cirrhotic patients with chronic hepatitis D is ~3%.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.title: Chronic Hepatitis C and Risk of Cirrhosis
items.content: A significant proportion of individuals remain chronically infected after acute HCV infection, with a notable risk of developing cirrhosis over time. The advent of DAA therapy has reduced the incidence of related complications.
keyPoints: 85-90% of patients remain chronically infected post-acute HCV infection.
20% may develop cirrhosis within 10-20 years; up to 50% in referral center series.
Annual risk of hepatic decompensation in cirrhotic chronic hepatitis C is ~4%; hepatocellular carcinoma risk ranges from 1-4%, with a recent report at 3.36%.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.title: Identifying Non-Viral Causes of Hepatitis-Like Symptoms
items.content: When diagnosing hepatitis-like symptoms, consider non-viral causes such as drug-induced liver injury and infections with agents like Leptospira or Candida. A thorough drug history is crucial to differentiate these from viral hepatitis.
keyPoints: Drug-induced liver injury can mimic acute hepatitis or cholestasis.
Infections with Leptospira, Candida, Brucella, Mycobacteria, and Pneumocystis can cause liver injury similar to viral hepatitis.
A complete drug history is essential for differential diagnosis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Distinguishing Alcohol-Associated Hepatitis from Viral Hepatitis
items.content: Alcohol-associated hepatitis (AH) should be considered when serum aminotransferase levels are not markedly elevated. A heavy drinking history and liver biopsy findings can help differentiate AH from viral hepatitis.
keyPoints: Serum aminotransferase levels in AH are generally not much above 200 IU/mL, almost always below 400 IU/mL.
A heavy drinking history (at least 40 g/d in women and 60 g/d in men for at least 6 months) is indicative of AH.
Liver biopsy findings such as fatty infiltration and alcoholic hyaline suggest alcohol-induced liver injury.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Recognizing Conditions Mimicking Acute Viral Hepatitis in Pregnancy
items.content: Conditions like acute fatty liver of pregnancy, cholestasis of pregnancy, eclampsia, and HELLP syndrome can mimic viral hepatitis during pregnancy. Clinical features are key to distinguishing these conditions.
keyPoints: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy, cholestasis of pregnancy, eclampsia, and HELLP syndrome can present similarly to acute viral hepatitis.
Clinical differentiation is crucial in pregnant patients presenting with hepatitis-like symptoms.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Management of Acute Hepatitis B
items.content: Most patients with acute hepatitis B recover spontaneously, and antiviral therapy is generally not required. However, in severe cases such as fulminant liver failure or a prolonged course (>4 weeks), treatment with nucleoside analogues like entecavir or tenofovir is recommended.
keyPoints: Recovery occurs in ~99% of previously healthy adults with acute hepatitis B.
Antiviral therapy is not typically necessary but may be considered for severe cases.
Treatment should continue until 3 months after HBsAg seroconversion or 6 months after HBeAg seroconversion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.title: Approach to Acute Hepatitis C
items.content: Most cases of acute hepatitis C progress to chronic infection, with spontaneous recovery occurring in only ~15–20% of patients. Early treatment with direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) is recommended rather than waiting for potential spontaneous recovery.
keyPoints: Spontaneous recovery occurs in ~15–20% of acute hepatitis C cases.
Early treatment with DAAs, a standard 8- to 12-week course, is advised.
Specific populations such as health care workers and injection drug users should be prioritized for early antiviral therapy.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.title: General Management of Acute Viral Hepatitis
items.content: In typical cases of acute viral hepatitis, specific treatment is often unnecessary. Supportive care includes rest, a high-calorie diet, and avoiding hepatotoxic drugs. Hospitalization may be required for severe illness.
keyPoints: Specific antiviral therapy is generally not needed in most cases.
Supportive measures include restricted physical activity and a high-calorie diet.
Avoid drugs that can cause cholestasis or are metabolized by the liver.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.title: Isolation and Precautionary Measures for Acute Viral Hepatitis
items.content: Physical isolation of patients with acute viral hepatitis is generally unnecessary except in specific cases such as fecal incontinence for hepatitis A and E, or uncontrolled bleeding for hepatitis B (with or without D) and C. Standard universal precautions, including blood precautions for hepatitis B and C, are sufficient.
keyPoints: Physical isolation rarely necessary except for specific conditions like fecal incontinence or uncontrolled bleeding.
HAV transmission risk is low during hospitalization; enteric precautions not recommended beyond standard hygiene.
Blood precautions emphasized for hepatitis B and C (avoid direct contact with blood/body fluids).
Hand washing and universal precautions are critical for all patients.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 2)

items.title: Management of Fulminant Hepatitis
items.content: In fulminant hepatitis, intensive care focusing on fluid balance, nutrition, circulation, respiration, bleeding control, hypoglycemia correction, and infection management is crucial. Liver transplantation is increasingly effective for these patients. Ribavirin therapy has shown success in severe acute hepatitis E but is contraindicated in pregnant women due to teratogenicity.
keyPoints: Intensive care with vigilant monitoring for infections and bleeding complications is essential.
Glucocorticoid therapy and other experimental treatments have not proven effective.
Liver transplantation shows excellent results in fulminant hepatitis cases.
Ribavirin (600 mg twice daily, 15 mg/kg) can be successful for acute hepatitis E; however, it's contraindicated in pregnant women due to teratogenicity.
In organ-transplant recipients with hepatitis E, reducing immunosuppression or switching drugs may eradicate HEV without antivirals.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 2)

items.title: Hepatitis A Prophylaxis: Transition from IG to Vaccine
items.content: The prophylactic strategy for hepatitis A has shifted from passive immunization with immunoglobulin (IG) to active immunization using vaccines. This change is due to the durability and simplicity of vaccine administration compared to IG, which offers limited protection duration.
keyPoints: Passive immunization with IG provides 80–90% effectiveness at preventing infection for 12–20 weeks.
For postexposure prophylaxis, IG (0.1 mL/kg) should be administered within 14 days of exposure.
Hepatitis A vaccine is now recommended over IG for all persons aged ≥12 months for both postexposure and preexposure prophylaxis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.title: Updated Recommendations for Hepatitis A Prophylaxis
items.content: In 2020, recommendations were updated to favor hepatitis A vaccine over IG for postexposure and preexposure prophylaxis. The vaccine is recommended for all persons aged ≥12 months, while IG remains an option for those with contraindications or infants under 12 months.
keyPoints: Hepatitis A vaccine is preferred for persons aged ≥12 months for both postexposure and preexposure prophylaxis.
For infants aged 6–11 months traveling internationally, the hepatitis A vaccine should be administered but not counted towards the universal childhood vaccination schedule.
IG (0.1 mL/kg) remains recommended for postexposure prophylaxis in persons with contraindications to vaccination and infants <12 months.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.title: Specific Prophylactic Measures for Hepatitis A
items.content: Prophylactic measures for hepatitis A vary based on age, exposure risk, and travel. IG is used in specific scenarios such as infants under 12 months or those with contraindications to vaccination, while the vaccine is recommended for most other cases.
keyPoints: For postexposure prophylaxis of immunocompromised adults and persons with chronic liver disease, both hepatitis A vaccination and IG (0.1 mL/kg) are advised.
Preexposure prophylaxis for infants <6 months involves IG at 0.1 mg/kg for travel up to 1 month, 0.2 mg/kg for up to 2 months, and repeat every 2 months thereafter.
IG is primarily used in outbreak scenarios where early containment is critical.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.title: Hepatitis A Vaccination Indications
items.content: The hepatitis A vaccine is recommended for various high-risk groups to prevent infection. These include children in areas with high HAV incidence, military personnel, populations with cyclic outbreaks like Alaskan natives, and individuals with chronic liver disease.
keyPoints: Hepatitis A vaccines are approved for persons at least 1 year old.
Protective levels of anti-HAV last at least 20 years after vaccination.
Routine vaccination recommended for all children since 2006 by the U.S. Public Health Service.

High-risk groups include military personnel, Alaskan natives, day-care employees, primate handlers, laboratory workers, and those with chronic liver disease (e.g., aminotransferase elevations ≥ 2 times upper limit of normal).
Patients with chronic hepatitis C, B, HIV infection, and nonalcoholic liver disease are candidates for vaccination.
Other high-risk groups: men who have sex with men, drug users, homeless persons, travelers to endemic areas, contacts of HAV cases, adoptees from high-endemicity countries.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.title: Hepatitis A Vaccine Efficacy and Timing
items.content: The hepatitis A vaccine provides long-lasting protection starting four weeks after the primary dose. It is effective in preventing secondary cases in households and daycare centers, contributing to a decline in mortality from hepatitis A.
keyPoints: Protection begins 4 weeks after primary inoculation.
Protective levels of anti-HAV last at least 20 years.
Effective in preventing secondary household and daycare center-associated acute hepatitis A cases.
Reported mortality from hepatitis A declined with vaccine use.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.title: Hepatitis B Vaccination Strategy
items.content: The strategy for preventing hepatitis B has evolved from passive immunoprophylaxis to active immunization using recombinant vaccines. Universal birth vaccination against HBV is standard since 2002, with the first dose recommended within 12 hours of delivery.
keyPoints: Passive immunoprophylaxis was based on IG and HBIG before 1982.
First active vaccine introduced in 1982; recombinant vaccines followed.
Universal birth vaccination against HBV has been standard since 2002.
Birth dose recommended within 12 hours of delivery.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.title: Universal Hepatitis B Vaccination Strategy
items.content: In low-endemicity areas like the United States, universal vaccination is recommended to reduce HBV infection rates. Targeting high-risk groups alone was ineffective, with less than 10% of these individuals being vaccinated and about 30% of acute hepatitis cases occurring outside these groups.
keyPoints: Universal vaccination recommended for all unvaccinated persons aged 19–59 years in the U.S.
<10% of high-risk targeted persons were actually vaccinated
~30% of sporadic acute hepatitis B cases did not fall into any high-risk group
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.title: Heplisav-B Vaccine Efficacy and Use
items.content: Heplisav-B, a two-injection vaccine, is approved for adults aged 18 or older. It shows higher seroprotection rates compared to Engerix-B in various age groups and is useful for revaccination of non-responders.
keyPoints: 95% protective levels with Heplisav-B vs. 81% with Engerix-B in adults aged 18–55 or 18–70
90% protection in older adults aged 40–70 and those with type 2 diabetes, compared to 71% and 65% respectively for Engerix-B
Approved for adults ≥18 years, should be avoided during pregnancy
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.title: PreHevbrio Vaccine Advantages
items.content: PreHevbrio includes all three hepatitis B surface antigen proteins and achieves higher seroprotection rates compared to Engerix-B, especially in older adults. It is the only vaccine not contraindicated for those with a yeast allergy.
keyPoints: Contains S, pre-S1, and pre-S2 antigens
89% seroprotection rate in persons ≥45 years compared to 73.1% with an old standard vaccine
Approved by FDA on December 1, 2021, for adults aged ≥18 years
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.title: HBIG and Hepatitis B Vaccine Protocol for Newborns
items.content: Newborns of HBsAg-positive mothers should receive immediate prophylaxis to prevent hepatitis B transmission. This involves administering a single dose of HBIG followed by a complete course of recombinant hepatitis B vaccine.
keyPoints: HBIG dosage: 0.5 mL IM in the thigh immediately after birth
Hepatitis B vaccine: start within first 12 hours of life
Vaccination is crucial for preventing perinatal transmission
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.title: Postexposure Prophylaxis for HBV Exposure in Adults
items.content: For adults exposed to HBsAg-positive blood or body fluids, immediate administration of HBIG followed by a hepatitis B vaccine course is recommended. Testing for anti-HBs helps guide further management.
keyPoints: HBIG dosage: 0.06 mL/kg IM as soon after exposure as possible
Vaccine course to begin within the first week post-exposure
Testing for anti-HBs in adults can determine immunity and need for booster doses
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.title: Hepatitis E Vaccine Efficacy and Availability
items.content: Two recombinant genotype 1 capsid protein vaccines have shown high efficacy against hepatitis E. The Chinese vaccine, Hecolin, is available in China but not FDA approved or available in the U.S.
keyPoints: Hecolin: 100% 12-month efficacy, licensed in China since 2011
Second vaccine by GlaxoSmithKline and U.S. Army: 96% 12-month efficacy
Vaccines are not FDA approved or available in the United States
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.title: Expanding Anti-HCV Testing for Broader Population
items.content: Anti-HCV testing recommendations have evolved to include all individuals aged 18 years and older, regardless of risk factors. This broadened scope aids in identifying asymptomatic cases and potential transmission risks.
keyPoints: Previously recommended for persons born between 1945 and 1965
Now expanded to all persons 18 years or older, independent of risk factors
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 5)

items.title: Strategies for Global HCV Eradication by 2030
items.content: Aiming for global eradication of hepatitis C by 2030 involves increased surveillance, immediate treatment upon diagnosis, and prompt initiation of therapy, especially among people who inject drugs.
keyPoints: Global eradication goal set for 2030
Requires higher rates of global surveillance
Immediate reflex treatment in persons testing positive for HCV
Undelayed, streamlined initiation of treatment
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 5)

Two structural capsid polypeptides and eight nonstructural proteins

Three structural capsid polypeptides and seven nonstructural proteins

items.question: What does the envelope protein HBsAg represent?

items.question: What role does HBxAg play in HBV pathogenesis?

It promotes viral replication and hepatocellular carcinoma.

items.question: Which immunoglobulin class of anti-HBc predominates during the first 6 months after acute HBV infection?

items.question: In chronic HBV infection, what is the typical duration for which HBsAg remains detectable?

items.question: During which phase of chronic HBV infection is HBeAg a qualitative marker?

items.question: What form does HBV DNA take in hepatocyte nuclei during the nonreplicative phase?

items.question: Which mutation in the precore region of HBV results in an HBeAg-negative phenotype by introducing a stop codon?

A single base substitution from C to T at nucleotide 1896

A mutation leading to a glycine to arginine substitution at position 145

items.question: In which regions of the HBV genome are mutations that prevent the expression of HBeAg commonly found?

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B due to precore mutations?

Low levels of HBV DNA (usually ≤10⁵ IU/mL)
Absence of any detectable HBV DNA

items.question: Which genotype of HBV in the United States is less prone to the G1896A mutation?

items.question: Which receptor do both HBV and HDV use to enter hepatocytes?

items.question: Which region of the hepatitis C virus genome is highly conserved among genotypes?

The envelope proteins E1 and E2

items.question: Which protein is necessary for the efficient assembly and release of HCV?

items.question: What is the role of NS3-4A serine protease in the hepatitis C virus life cycle?

Cleaves all downstream proteins from the polyprotein

items.question: Which cell type is central to both viral clearance and liver injury in hepatitis B infection?

items.question: What suggests that HBV is not directly cytopathic to hepatocytes?

High levels of HBsAg in blood

items.question: Which immune cells are involved in the early innate immune response to HBV infection?

items.question: What mechanism may explain why neonatal HBV infection often leads to chronic, lifelong infection?
options: In utero exposure to HBeAg induces T-cell tolerance to nucleocapsid proteins.
Neonates have a robust immune response that clears the virus quickly.
HBV-specific T cells are effectively primed during in utero exposure.
Hepatic necroinflammatory activity is high immediately after birth.

items.question: What is a key difference between neonatal HBV infection and adult-acquired HBV infection?
options: Neonatal infection leads to acute hepatitis, while adult infection does not.
Adult-acquired infection often results in chronicity and high risk of hepatocellular carcinoma.
Neonatal infection is associated with robust liver injury immediately after birth.
Both neonatal and adult infections result in similar levels of immunologic tolerance.
items.explanation: Adult-acquired HBV infection typically results in a strong immune response, acute hepatitis, and low risk of chronicity or hepatocellular carcinoma.

items.question: What phase is characterized by high-level HBV replication with minimal inflammatory liver injury?

items.question: What is the typical clinical outcome of adult-acquired HBV infection?

Self-limited acute hepatitis B with low risk of chronicity
Immediate establishment of immunologic tolerance and chronic infection
High-level liver injury during the neonatal period
items.explanation: Adult-acquired HBV infection usually results in self-limited acute hepatitis, with a robust immune response and low risk of chronicity.

items.question: Which cytokine gene polymorphism is most strongly associated with self-limited hepatitis C?

items.question: What mechanism allows HCV to evade immune-mediated clearance?

Upregulation of inhibitory receptors on NK cells
Enhanced IFN-γ elaboration by NK cells
items.explanation: HCV evades immune-mediated clearance through mutations in CD8+ T cell-targeted viral epitopes.
