items.question: Which cells are dysfunctional in persistent HCV infection?

items.question: What is the role of CD4+ helper T cells in HCV-associated liver injury?

They stimulate virus-specific CD8+ cytotoxic T cells

items.question: Which of the following extrahepatic manifestations is most commonly associated with chronic HBV infection rather than acute HBV infection?

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.question: What was the estimated range of transfusion-associated hepatitis C risk after improved screening measures?
items.explanation: The passage states that the risk of transfusion-associated hepatitis C was reduced to levels ranging between 1 in 2.3 million and 1 in 4.7 million transfusions.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.question: Which population had the highest prevalence of HCV infection according to NHANES survey data?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that in the NHANES survey conducted between 1999 and 2002, the peak age decile for HCV infection prevalence shifted to those aged 40–49 years.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.question: What was one of the major reasons for the high frequency of HCV infection in Egypt?
options: Contaminated equipment used for medical procedures
items.explanation: The passage attributes the high frequency of HCV infection in Egypt to contaminated equipment used for medical procedures and unsafe injection practices.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.question: Which population had an estimated HCV infection prevalence of 10.7% according to NHANES data from 2013–2016?
options: Domiciled and noninstitutionalized U.S. civilian population
items.explanation: The prevalence among both incarcerated and homeless persons was estimated to be 10.7%.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.question: What age group is associated with more than 70% of HCV-associated deaths?
items.explanation: >70% of HCV-associated deaths occurred in the 'baby boomer' cohort born between 1945 and 1965.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.question: Which HCV genotype is most common in the United States?
items.explanation: In the United States, genotype 1 accounts for 70% of HCV infections.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.question: What was the estimated annual mortality from hepatitis C in the United States before high-efficacy DAA therapy?
items.explanation: Hepatitis C was estimated to account for 8000–10,000 deaths per year in the United States.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.question: Which of the following groups has been reported to have isolated clusters of sexually transmitted HCV infection?
options: HIV-infected men who have sex with men
items.explanation: Isolated clusters of sexually transmitted HCV infection have been reported in HIV-infected men who have sex with men.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.question: What is the estimated efficiency of HCV transmission through accidental needle punctures in health workers?
items.explanation: The efficiency of HCV transmission through accidental needle punctures is ~3%.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.question: What is the estimated rate of perinatal transmission of HCV in babies born to HIV-HCV coinfected mothers?
items.explanation: The high risk of perinatal transmission is typically in excess of 10% for babies born to HIV-HCV coinfected mothers.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.question: Which population has seen an increase in HCV prevalence due to the opioid epidemic?
items.explanation: The prevalence of HCV infection in women aged 15–44 years has doubled, attributed to the opioid epidemic.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)
items.question: Which genotypes of HEV are primarily associated with large waterborne outbreaks in endemic areas?
items.explanation: Large waterborne outbreaks in endemic areas are linked to genotypes 1 and 2.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)
items.question: What was the estimated worldwide annual incidence of acute HEV infections?
items.explanation: The worldwide annual incidence of acute HEV infections has been estimated conservatively to be at least 20 million.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)
items.question: In nonendemic areas, what is the primary mode of HEV transmission that accounts for mostly subclinical infections?
items.explanation: In nonendemic areas, a zoonotic reservoir for HEV primarily in swine accounts for mostly subclinical infections.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)

items.question: What is the typical incubation period for hepatitis A?
items.explanation: The incubation period for hepatitis A ranges from 15 to 45 days.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: Which type of acute viral hepatitis is more likely to present with a low-grade fever?
items.explanation: A low-grade fever is more often present in hepatitis A and E than in hepatitis B or C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: During which phase of acute viral hepatitis do constitutional symptoms usually diminish?
items.explanation: With the onset of clinical jaundice, the constitutional prodromal symptoms usually diminish.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: What is the likelihood of jaundice in acute hepatitis B compared to acute hepatitis C?
items.explanation: Jaundice is substantially more common in acute hepatitis B than in acute hepatitis C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.question: When does jaundice typically become visible in patients with acute viral hepatitis?
options: When the serum bilirubin value is >85 μmol/L
When the serum bilirubin value is >43 μmol/L
When the serum aminotransferase levels peak
When the serum bilirubin level falls
items.explanation: Jaundice becomes visible when the serum bilirubin exceeds 43 μmol/L.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: What is a common feature of acute viral hepatitis regarding serum globulin levels?
options: A significant decrease in the $ γ $ globulin fraction
An elevation in the $ γ $ globulin fraction
No change in the $ γ $ globulin fraction
items.explanation: A diffuse but mild elevation of the $ γ $ globulin fraction is common during acute viral hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: What does a prolonged prothrombin time (PT) in patients with acute viral hepatitis indicate?
options: Severe hepatic synthetic defect and worse prognosis
items.explanation: A prolonged PT may reflect a severe hepatic synthetic defect, signify extensive hepatocellular necrosis, and indicate a worse prognosis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: What is the significance of detecting IgM anti-HAV in patients with acute illness?
options: It indicates a diagnosis of hepatitis A
It rules out other viral infections
It suggests an autoimmune liver disease
items.explanation: Detection of IgM anti-HAV during acute illness is used to diagnose hepatitis A.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: What is the principal clinical usefulness of HBeAg in patients with hepatitis B?
options: It indicates the presence of anti-HBs.
It serves as an indicator of relative infectivity.
It helps distinguish between acute and chronic infection.
It confirms the presence of IgM anti-HBc.
items.explanation: HBeAg is used as an indicator of relative infectivity in hepatitis B patients.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.question: In the context of chronic HBV infection, what does the presence of both HBsAg and anti-HBs indicate?
options: The patient is likely to clear HBsAg imminently.
The patient has a higher risk of acute reactivation.
The patient tends to have advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis.
The patient shows no signs of liver injury.
items.explanation: Patients with both HBsAg and anti-HBs tend to have higher aminotransferase levels, are older, and more likely to have advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.question: Which serologic marker is detected after immunization with the hepatitis B vaccine?
items.explanation: After vaccination, anti-HBs is the only serologic marker to appear.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.question: What does a high level of serum HBV DNA indicate in patients with chronic hepatitis B?
options: Increased risk of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.explanation: High levels of serum HBV DNA increase the risk of cirrhosis, hepatic decompensation, and hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.question: Which test represents the 'gold standard' for establishing a diagnosis of hepatitis C?
items.explanation: Assays for HCV RNA are the most sensitive and represent the 'gold standard' in establishing a diagnosis of hepatitis C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.question: What does detectable anti-HCV in the absence of HCV RNA signify?
options: Spontaneous or therapeutically induced recovery from hepatitis C
items.explanation: Detectable anti-HCV in the absence of HCV RNA signifies spontaneous or therapeutically induced recovery from ('cured') hepatitis C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.question: What is a reliable indicator of recent HBV infection when both HBsAg and anti-HDV are present?
items.explanation: In simultaneous acute HBV and HDV infections, IgM anti-HBc will be detectable, indicating recent infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.question: What is the role of liver biopsy in acute viral hepatitis?
options: It is routinely performed to confirm diagnosis.
It is rarely necessary unless there's a questionable diagnosis or alternate chronic hepatitis evidence.
It is used to determine HCV genotype.
items.explanation: Liver biopsy is rarely necessary in acute viral hepatitis, except when the diagnosis is questionable or suggests an alternate diagnosis of chronic hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.question: Which serologic test can be used to diagnose acute hepatitis B in the absence of HBsAg?
items.explanation: A diagnosis of acute hepatitis B can be made when IgM anti-HBc is detectable, even in the absence of HBsAg.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.question: In a patient with acute hepatitis illness, which test supports a diagnosis of acute hepatitis C?
items.explanation: The presence of anti-HCV supports the diagnosis of acute hepatitis C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.question: When evaluating a patient with suspected acute viral hepatitis, which serologic test is NOT routinely included in the initial panel?
items.explanation: Testing for Hepatitis E antibody is not part of the routine initial serologic panel; it's considered if other tests are negative and risk factors exist.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.question: Which condition can be excluded by negative serologic testing for hepatitis B in a patient with acute hepatitis?
items.explanation: Negative serologic testing for hepatitis B excludes the diagnosis of acute hepatitis D.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.question: Which of the following factors suggest a poorer prognosis in patients with acute hepatitis B?
options: Young age and no underlying medical disorders
Initial presenting features such as ascites, peripheral edema, and symptoms of hepatic encephalopathy
Low serum bilirubin values and normal PT
items.explanation: Ascites, peripheral edema, and symptoms of hepatic encephalopathy suggest a poorer prognosis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROGNOSIS

items.question: What is the approximate case-fatality rate for hepatitis A in previously healthy individuals?
items.explanation: The case-fatality rate for hepatitis A and B is very low (~0.1%).
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROGNOSIS

items.question: In which population does the case-fatality rate of waterborne hepatitis E reach up to 10-20%?
items.explanation: The case-fatality rate of waterborne hepatitis E is up to 10-20% in pregnant women.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of relapsing hepatitis A?
items.explanation: Relapsing hepatitis A is characterized by recurrence of symptoms, aminotransferase elevations, occasional jaundice, and fecal excretion of HAV.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.question: Which complication occurs primarily in hepatitis B, D, and E but rarely in hepatitis A?
items.explanation: Fulminant hepatitis is the most feared complication of acute viral hepatitis and occurs primarily in hepatitis B, D, and E.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.question: Which condition may develop during the prodromal phase of acute hepatitis B?
items.explanation: A serum sickness-like syndrome characterized by arthralgia or arthritis, rash, angioedema, and occasionally hematuria and proteinuria may develop during the prodromal phase of acute hepatitis B.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.question: Which group is at a higher risk for chronic infection after acute HBV infection?
options: Neonates and persons with Down syndrome
items.explanation: The likelihood of remaining chronically infected after acute HBV infection is especially high among neonates, persons with Down syndrome, and other immunocompromised groups.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.question: Which feature is NOT indicative of progression from acute to chronic hepatitis B?
options: Persistence of HBeAg for more than 3 months
Return of serum aminotransferase levels to normal within 6–12 months
Lack of complete resolution of clinical symptoms such as anorexia and fatigue
Presence of bridging/interface or multilobular hepatic necrosis on liver biopsy
items.explanation: Failure, not return to normal, of serum aminotransferase levels within 6–12 months indicates progression to chronic hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.question: What is the annual rate of cirrhosis in patients with chronic hepatitis D?
items.explanation: The annual rate of cirrhosis in patients with chronic hepatitis D is reported as 4%.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.question: Which factor does NOT influence the progression of chronic hepatitis C?
items.explanation: HAV infection is not associated with the progression of chronic hepatitis C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.question: Which statement about hepatitis D superinfection in patients with chronic hepatitis B is true?
options: It decreases the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma.
It can transform mild chronic hepatitis B into severe, progressive disease.
It has no effect on the severity of chronic hepatitis B.
It reduces the likelihood of cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Hepatitis D superinfection can worsen chronic hepatitis B and increase the risk of severe outcomes like hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following tests may be helpful in differentiating viral hepatitis from other conditions if HBsAg, anti-HBc, IgM anti-HAV, and anti-HCV (and anti-HEV) determinations are negative?
items.explanation: The differential heterophile and serologic tests can help differentiate viral hepatitis from other conditions when specific hepatitis markers are negative.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common cause of liver injury that might be confused with acute viral hepatitis?
items.explanation: Hypothyroidism is not listed as a cause of liver injury that might be confused with acute viral hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In the context of differential diagnosis, what is an important consideration when evaluating patients with unexplained repeated episodes of acute hepatitis?
items.explanation: Unexplained repeated episodes of acute hepatitis may suggest an underlying disorder such as chronic hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition is characterized by serum aminotransferase levels generally not much above 200 IU/mL and requires a heavy drinking history for diagnosis?
items.explanation: Alcohol-associated hepatitis is characterized by serum aminotransferase levels generally not much above 200 IU/mL and requires a heavy drinking history.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which nucleoside analogues are recommended for severe, but not mild-moderate, acute hepatitis B?
items.explanation: Entecavir and tenofovir are the most potent and least resistance-prone agents recommended for severe acute hepatitis B.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical recovery rate in previously healthy adults with clinically apparent acute hepatitis B?
items.explanation: Recovery occurs in ~99% of previously healthy adults with clinically apparent acute hepatitis B.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.question: In the current era, what is advised for patients with acute hepatitis C instead of waiting for potential spontaneous recovery?
options: Early treatment with oral DAA therapy
items.explanation: Early treatment with a standard, full course of one of the first-line drug combinations approved for chronic hepatitis C is recommended.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.question: Which group should be treated if they identify acute hepatitis C after occupational accidents?
options: Health care workers with needle sticks
items.explanation: Health care workers who sustain hepatitis C-contaminated needle sticks should be treated if acute hepatitis C is identified.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.question: Which type of viral hepatitis might require physical isolation due to fecal incontinence?
items.explanation: Physical isolation is rarely necessary except for hepatitis A and E in cases of fecal incontinence.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 2)

items.question: What precautions are emphasized for patients with hepatitis B and C?
items.explanation: For hepatitis B and C, emphasis should be placed on blood precautions.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 2)

items.question: Which therapy has been shown to be ineffective in fulminant hepatitis?
items.explanation: Controlled trials have shown glucocorticoid therapy to be ineffective in fulminant hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 2)

items.question: Which form of prophylaxis for hepatitis A has supplanted IG in almost all cases?
items.explanation: Hepatitis A vaccine, which involves active immunization with killed vaccines, has supplanted IG in almost all cases.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.question: For post-exposure prophylaxis of persons aged <12 months or those with contraindications to hepatitis A vaccination, what is recommended?
items.explanation: For post-exposure prophylaxis in infants aged <12 months and those with contraindications to hepatitis A vaccination, IG at doses of 0.1 mL/kg is recommended.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended dose of IG for preexposure prophylaxis in infants aged <6 months traveling internationally?
options: 0.1 mg/kg for travel up to 2 months
0.2 mg/kg for travel durations up to 1 month
0.1 mg/kg for travel up to 1 month, 0.2 mg/kg for travel up to 2 months
0.3 mg/kg for travel longer than 2 months
items.explanation: For preexposure prophylaxis in infants aged <6 months traveling internationally, IG at a dose of 0.2 mg/kg is recommended for travel up to 1 month.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.question: Which group does NOT require postexposure prophylaxis for hepatitis A?
options: Intimate contacts of persons with hepatitis A
Casual contacts like office or school personnel
Persons without anti-HAV in their serum
items.explanation: Postexposure prophylaxis is not necessary for casual contacts such as those in offices, factories, schools, or hospitals.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following groups is NOT specifically mentioned as being at increased risk for HAV infection and candidates for hepatitis A vaccination?
items.explanation: Patients with diabetes mellitus are not specifically mentioned as being at increased risk for HAV infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.question: What is the minimum age recommended for hepatitis A vaccination according to the passage?
items.explanation: Hepatitis A vaccines are approved for use in persons who are at least 1 year old.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.question: When should the birth dose of hepatitis B vaccine be administered according to recent recommendations?
items.explanation: Recent recommendations suggest that the birth dose be administered within 12 hours of delivery.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.question: Which hepatitis vaccine was introduced first for active immunization?
items.explanation: The first vaccine for active immunization was prepared from purified, noninfectious, 22-nm spherical HBsAg particles derived from the plasma of healthy HBsAg carriers.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.question: Which age group did the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) recommend for universal hepatitis B vaccination in 2022?
options: All unvaccinated persons aged 60 years and older
Only those at high risk regardless of age
items.explanation: ACIP recommended universal vaccination for all unvaccinated persons aged 19–59 years.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.question: Which hepatitis B vaccine is not contraindicated in the presence of a yeast allergy?
items.explanation: PreHevbrio is the only vaccine not contraindicated in the presence of a yeast allergy.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.question: What is the recommended site for administering hepatitis B vaccines?
items.explanation: Hepatitis B vaccines are recommended to be administered in the deltoid muscle.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.question: Which hepatitis B vaccine activates toll-like 9 receptors?
items.explanation: Heplisav-B is a recombinant vaccine with a novel adjuvant that activates toll-like 9 receptors.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.question: What is the recommended initial dose of HBIG for newborns whose mothers are hepatitis B surface antigen-positive?
items.explanation: A single dose of 0.5 mL HBIG is recommended for newborns immediately after birth.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.question: For pregnant mothers with high-level HBV DNA, what additional treatment can reduce perinatal transmission?
items.explanation: Adding antiviral nucleoside analogues during the third trimester reduces perinatal transmission.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.question: When should a single IM dose of HBIG be administered to persons exposed by sexual contact to a patient with acute hepatitis B?
items.explanation: A single IM dose of HBIG should be given within 14 days of exposure.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.question: What is the recommended action for adults after completing a hepatitis B vaccine course?
items.explanation: Testing adults for anti-HBs after vaccination is advisable to document immunity.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.question: Which group is recommended for anti-HCV testing regardless of risk factors?
options: Only those with known exposure to HCV
items.explanation: Anti-HCV testing is now recommended for all persons 18 years or older, independent of risk factors.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 5)

items.question: What is the recommendation regarding breastfeeding for mothers with hepatitis C?
options: Breastfeeding is not addressed in the guidelines
items.explanation: No special precautions are recommended for babies born to mothers with hepatitis C, and breastfeeding does not have to be restricted.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 5)

items.question: What is the goal of the ambitious program toward HCV prevention by 2030?
options: Reduce HCV transmission in high-risk populations only
items.explanation: The ambitious goal is toward the global eradication of HCV by 2030.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 5)

items.statement: All human hepatitis viruses are RNA viruses.
items.explanation: Hepatitis B is a DNA virus, not an RNA virus.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis

items.statement: Severe acute hepatitis in children post-COVID-19 has been linked to adenovirus type 41.
items.explanation: Links have been reported of infection with adenovirus type 41 and severe acute hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis

items.statement: Hepatitis A virus can be cultivated reproducibly in vitro and primate models.
items.explanation: HAV can indeed be cultivated reproducibly in vitro and in primate models.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Hepatitis B virus replicates directly from a DNA template.
items.explanation: HBV relies on reverse transcription of minus-strand DNA from a pregenomic RNA intermediate, not direct replication from a DNA template.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: The clinical course and outcome of HBV infection are dependent on the subtype.
items.explanation: Clinical course and outcome are independent of subtype.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Pre-S proteins have significant clinical relevance in routine serologic testing for HBV.
items.explanation: Pre-S proteins have little clinical relevance and are not included in routine serologic testing repertoires.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Naked core particles circulate in the serum.
items.explanation: Naked core particles do not circulate in the serum as they are sequestered within an HBsAg coat.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 3)

items.statement: Anti-HBs becomes detectable after HBsAg disappears.
items.explanation: After HBsAg disappears, anti-HBs becomes detectable in serum.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 3)

items.statement: The presence of IgG anti-HBc indicates current or recent acute hepatitis B infection.
items.explanation: IgG anti-HBc is associated with remote past hepatitis B infection or chronic HBV infection, not current or recent acute infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 4)

items.statement: Anti-HBs provides protection against reinfection with HBV.
items.explanation: The presence of anti-HBs in serum is associated with immunity and protection against HBV reinfection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 4)

items.statement: In the nonreplicative phase of chronic HBV infection, liver injury tends to subside.
items.explanation: Liver injury tends to subside during the nonreplicative phase.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 5)

items.statement: High-titer IgM anti-HBc is a reliable marker to distinguish acute from chronic hepatitis B infection.
items.explanation: Relying on IgM anti-HBc versus IgG anti-HBc may not always be reliable for distinguishing between acute and chronic hepatitis B.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 5)

items.statement: Escape mutants of HBV result from a single amino acid substitution at position 145 in the immunodominant a determinant.
items.explanation: The glycine to arginine substitution at position 145 leads to escape mutants.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 6)

items.statement: Extrahepatic HBV infection is primarily associated with tissue injury in sites such as lymph nodes and bone marrow.
items.explanation: Although HBV antigens are found extrahepatically, they do not appear to cause tissue injury.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 6)

items.statement: HDV RNA can replicate independently of HBV.
items.explanation: Although intracellular replication of HDV RNA can occur without HBV, complete hepatitis D viroids and liver injury require the cooperative helper function of HBV.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 7)

items.statement: Genotype 2 of HDV is linked to severe acute disease.
items.explanation: Preliminarily, genotype 2 has been linked to milder disease and genotype 3 to severe acute disease.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 7)

items.statement: Hepatitis C virus integrates into the host genome.
items.explanation: HCV does not replicate via a DNA intermediate, so it does not integrate into the host genome.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 8)

items.statement: Neutralizing antibodies to HCV tend to provide lasting immunity against reinfection.
items.explanation: Neutralizing antibodies to HCV are short-lived and do not induce lasting immunity.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 8)

items.statement: The clinical relevance of HCV lymphocyte infection is well established.
items.explanation: The clinical relevance of HCV lymphocyte infection is not known.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 9)

items.statement: HCV RNA can be detected before the appearance of anti-HCV antibodies.
items.explanation: HCV RNA can indeed be detected within a few days after exposure, well before anti-HCV appears.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 9)

items.statement: HBV infection is directly cytopathic to hepatocytes.
items.explanation: Under ordinary circumstances, none of the hepatitis viruses are known to be directly cytopathic to hepatocytes.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: NK cells reduce helper CD4+ T cells during HBV infection.
items.explanation: NK cells reduce helper CD4+ cells, leading to reduced CD8+ cells and exhaustion of the virus-specific T-cell response.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: Neonatally acquired HBV infection is associated with high levels of immunologic tolerance.
items.explanation: Neonatal exposure to HBV leads to a high level of immunologic tolerance, resulting in chronic infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.statement: Adult-acquired HBV infection typically results in lifelong chronic hepatitis.
items.explanation: Adult-acquired HBV infection usually leads to a robust immune response and self-limited acute hepatitis, with low risk of chronicity.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.statement: HCV proteins enhance the immunoregulatory NF-κB pathway.
items.explanation: HCV core, NS4B, and NS5B suppress the NF-κB pathway.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.statement: NK cells show enhanced function in spontaneous clearance of HCV.
items.explanation: In spontaneous clearance, NK cells have enhanced IFN-γ elaboration and activating receptor expression.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.statement: Liver biopsy is routinely required for the diagnosis of acute viral hepatitis.
items.explanation: Clinical features and serologic testing are the cornerstone of diagnosis; liver biopsy is rarely needed.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EXTRAHEPATIC MANIFESTATIONS | PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Hepatitis E can cause renal complications due to direct extrahepatic-site infection.
items.explanation: Renal complications in hepatitis E may result from immunologic mechanisms or direct infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EXTRAHEPATIC MANIFESTATIONS | PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Hepatitis A is rarely transmitted through blood.
items.explanation: Although hepatitis A is primarily fecal-oral, it is rarely bloodborne.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: Hepatitis A infection in childhood leads to lifelong immunity.
items.explanation: Infection with hepatitis A typically results in lifelong immunity.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: Oral ingestion is a highly efficient route for hepatitis B transmission.
items.explanation: Oral ingestion has been documented as a potential but inefficient route of HBV exposure.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: Breast-feeding is contraindicated in women with hepatitis B.
items.explanation: Epidemiologic evidence suggests that perinatal transmission of HBV is not related to breast-feeding, which is not contraindicated.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: The prevalence of HBsAg seropositivity in children aged 6–19 years declined to as low as 0.03% following universal childhood vaccination.
items.explanation: Following the implementation of universal childhood vaccination, the prevalence of HBsAg seropositivity in children aged 6–19 years declined significantly.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.statement: Universal screening for hepatitis B was recommended by the CDC in 2023 due to its effectiveness in reducing prevalence among high-risk groups.
items.explanation: Universal screening was recommended because risk-group screening had not been effective, and most cases occurred outside these groups.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)
items.statement: The CDC recommends universal screening of all adults over 18 years old for hepatitis B.
items.explanation: In 2023, the CDC recommended universal screening for all adults over 18.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)
items.statement: Hemodialysis patients have no increased risk of HDV infection.
items.explanation: Hemodialysis patients are listed as having an increased risk for HDV infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.statement: The overall frequency of reported hepatitis C cases decreased in the 1990s due to reduced new cases among injection drug users.
items.explanation: The passage explains that the overall frequency of reported hepatitis C cases fell by 80% in the 1990s, paralleling a reduction in new cases among injection drug users.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.statement: The prevalence of HCV infection decreased significantly after the introduction of second-generation HCV screening tests in 1992.
items.explanation: Despite the introduction of second-generation HCV screening tests, the prevalence was sustained by an aging cohort who acquired infections earlier.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.statement: The prevalence of HCV infection among domiciled and noninstitutionalized U.S. civilian population was estimated to be higher than that among incarcerated and homeless persons.
items.explanation: The prevalence among incarcerated and homeless persons was significantly higher at 10.7%.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.statement: HCV genotype 1 is the most common worldwide.
items.explanation: Worldwide, genotype 1 is indeed the most common HCV genotype.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.statement: Breastfeeding increases the risk of HCV transmission from an infected mother to her infant.
items.explanation: Breast-feeding does not increase the risk of HCV infection between an infected mother and her infant.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.statement: The annual incidence of new HCV infections has been consistently decreasing.
items.explanation: Although the overall incidence has continued to fall, the rate of new infections increased since 2002 and accelerated since 2010.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)
items.statement: HEV infection is the most common cause of acute viral hepatitis worldwide.
items.explanation: HEV infection is estimated to be the most common cause of acute viral hepatitis with at least 20 million annual cases.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)
items.statement: The NHANES III survey found that anti-HEV prevalence remained constant in the United States over two decades.
items.explanation: A repeat NHANES study showed a substantial reduction in anti-HEV from 21% to 6%, indicating a decrease.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 5)

items.statement: Splenomegaly and cervical adenopathy are present in all patients with acute viral hepatitis.
items.explanation: Splenomegaly and cervical adenopathy are present in only 10–20% of patients with acute hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: Acute HDV infection can occur without the presence of HBV.
items.explanation: Infection with HDV requires the presence of acute or chronic HBV infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: The level of serum aminotransferases correlates well with the degree of liver cell damage.
items.explanation: The level of serum aminotransferases does not correlate well with the degree of liver cell damage.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: Serum IgM levels are characteristically elevated during acute hepatitis A.
items.explanation: The serum IgM level is more characteristically elevated during acute hepatitis A.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: Anti-HBs is commonly detectable in patients with acute hepatitis B.
items.explanation: Anti-HBs is rarely detectable in the presence of HBsAg in patients with acute hepatitis B.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.statement: IgM anti-HCV assays are clinically valuable for diagnosing acute hepatitis C.
items.explanation: Assays specific for IgM anti-HCV have never been found to be of clinical value.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.statement: HCV RNA can be detected before the appearance of anti-HCV in patients with acute hepatitis C.
items.explanation: HCV RNA can indeed be detected even before the acute elevation of aminotransferase activity and before the appearance of anti-HCV.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.statement: The presence of HDV infection is best confirmed by detecting serum HDV RNA or antigen.
items.explanation: Serum HDV RNA or antigen can be detected early during acute coinfection, confirming HDV infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.statement: The presence of HBsAg, with or without IgM anti-HBc, indicates an acute HBV infection.
items.explanation: HBsAg with IgM anti-HBc indicates acute HBV; without IgM anti-HBc, it suggests chronic HBV.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.statement: Testing for HCV RNA is unnecessary if anti-HCV is detectable in a patient with suspected acute hepatitis C.
items.explanation: HCV RNA testing helps establish the diagnosis when anti-HCV is undetectable, despite suspicion of acute hepatitis C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.statement: Hepatitis C is more severe during the acute phase than hepatitis B.
items.explanation: Hepatitis C is less severe during the acute phase than hepatitis B.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROGNOSIS

items.statement: The case-fatality rate for simultaneous acute hepatitis B and D coinfection can be around 5% in outbreaks among injection drug users.
items.explanation: In several outbreaks of acute simultaneous HBV and HDV infection among injection drug users, the case-fatality rate was ~5%.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROGNOSIS

items.statement: Hepatitis A can progress to chronic liver disease.
items.explanation: Even when complications occur, hepatitis A remains self-limited and does not progress to chronic liver disease.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.statement: Fulminant hepatitis is common in hepatitis C.
items.explanation: Fulminant hepatitis is hardly ever seen in hepatitis C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.statement: HAV infection causes chronic liver disease in immunocompetent hosts.
items.explanation: HAV does not cause chronic liver disease in immunocompetent individuals.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.statement: The annual risk of hepatocellular carcinoma in cirrhotic patients with chronic hepatitis C is about 3.36%.
items.explanation: A definitive report states the annual risk of hepatocellular carcinoma in these patients as 3.36%.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.statement: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy can be confused with acute viral hepatitis.
items.explanation: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is one of the conditions that may mimic acute viral hepatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Viral hepatitis in the elderly is often misdiagnosed as chronic pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Viral hepatitis in the elderly is more commonly misdiagnosed as obstructive jaundice from a common duct stone or carcinoma of the pancreas, not chronic pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Glucocorticoid therapy is beneficial in severe cases of acute viral hepatitis.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoid therapy has no value and may be deleterious, increasing the risk of chronicity.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.statement: Most cases of acute viral hepatitis require specific antiviral treatment.
items.explanation: In most cases of typical acute viral hepatitis, specific treatment is generally not necessary.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.statement: Enteric precautions are necessary for all patients with viral hepatitis.
items.explanation: Enteric precautions are unnecessary except in specific cases like fecal incontinence for hepatitis A and E.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 2)

items.statement: Ribavirin is contraindicated in pregnant women with fulminant hepatitis E.
items.explanation: Ribavirin, which is teratogenic, cannot be used in pregnant women with fulminant hepatitis E.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 2)

items.statement: Passive immunization with IG provides lifelong protection against hepatitis A.
items.explanation: Passive immunization with IG provides only 12–20 weeks of protection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.statement: Active immunization with vaccine is the current preferred approach for hepatitis A prevention.
items.explanation: The passage states that active immunization with vaccines is now preferable to IG for preventing hepatitis A.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.statement: Hepatitis A vaccine is recommended for pregnant women at risk of infection.
items.explanation: The passage states that hepatitis A vaccine is now recommended as well for pregnant women at risk of infection or severe outcomes from infection during pregnancy.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.statement: Hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) has been proven to prevent HBV infection.
items.explanation: The efficacy of HBIG in preventing infection is questioned, and its contribution appears to be in reducing the frequency of clinical illness.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.statement: Pregnancy is a contraindication to hepatitis B vaccination.
items.explanation: Pregnancy is not a contraindication to hepatitis B vaccination.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.statement: Universal vaccination of children in HBV-hyperendemic areas has led to an increase in liver-related mortality.
items.explanation: Universal vaccination of children in HBV-hyperendemic areas has resulted in a marked decline in complications, including liver-related mortality.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.statement: Postvaccination anti-HBs testing of children is recommended.
items.explanation: Testing for anti-HBs in children is not recommended due to nearly universal immunogenicity.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.statement: IG derived from HEV-endemic populations is effective in preventing hepatitis E.
items.explanation: IG derived from HEV-endemic populations does not appear to be effective for hepatitis E prevention.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.statement: Sexual transmission of hepatitis C is unlikely in stable, monogamous sexual partners.
items.explanation: For stable, monogamous sexual partners, sexual transmission of hepatitis C is indeed considered unlikely.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 5)

items.statement: Barrier precautions are recommended for all individuals with hepatitis C.
items.explanation: Barrier precautions are specifically recommended for persons with multiple sexual partners or sexually transmitted diseases, not universally for all individuals with hepatitis C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 5)

items.assertion: Hepatitis A virus has six genotypes affecting humans.
items.reason: Despite nucleotide sequence variation, all strains of HAV are immunologically indistinguishable and belong to one serotype.
items.explanation: The assertion is true as there are six genotypes recognized; however, the reason does not explain this fact.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Hepatitis B virus has a compact genomic structure.
items.reason: HBV DNA codes for four sets of viral products with overlapping genes S, C, P, and X.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: HBeAg is a marker of ongoing viral replication.
items.reason: HBeAg is secreted into the circulation as a product of the C gene's precore region.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 3)

items.assertion: HBxAg is routinely tested in clinical practice.
items.reason: It plays a significant role in promoting viral replication and hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, although the reason is true; testing for HBxAg is not part of routine clinical practice.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 3)

items.assertion: Isolated anti-HBc always indicates active virus replication.
items.reason: Most instances of isolated anti-HBc represent hepatitis B infection in the remote past, not necessarily indicating active virus replication.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because isolated anti-HBc does not always indicate active virus replication.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 4)

items.assertion: HBeAg appears concurrently with or shortly after HBsAg during acute HBV infection.
items.reason: The appearance of HBeAg coincides with high levels of virus replication and the presence of circulating intact virions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 4)

items.assertion: Precore-mutant HBV can lead to more severe liver disease progression.
items.reason: The mutation results in an HBeAg-negative phenotype, which is associated with rapid progression to cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the precore mutation leads to a phenotype linked with more severe liver disease.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 6)

items.assertion: The G1896A mutation is common in HBV genotype A.
items.reason: HBV genotype A is prevalent in the United States, where this mutation is less frequent.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; genotype A is less prone to the G1896A mutation.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 6)

items.assertion: HDV relies absolutely on HBV for its replication.
items.reason: The duration of HDV infection is determined by the duration of HBV infection and cannot outlast it.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HDV relies on HBV.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 7)

items.assertion: HDV antigen is expressed primarily in hepatocyte nuclei.
items.reason: This expression results from HDV's replication mechanism involving host RNA polymerase II.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why HDV antigen is expressed primarily in hepatocyte nuclei.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 7)

items.assertion: Hepatitis C virus can evade host immunologic containment.
items.reason: The envelope proteins are coded for by the hypervariable region, which varies from isolate to isolate.
items.explanation: The variability of the envelope proteins allows HCV to evade host immune responses.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 8)

items.assertion: Hepatitis C virus replication is limited by robust animal models.
items.reason: A tractable immunocompetent murine model has remained elusive.
items.explanation: The lack of a suitable animal model hinders the development of vaccines for HCV.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · VIROLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 8)

items.assertion: A robust cytolytic T-cell response is necessary for recovery from HBV infection.
items.reason: HBV-HLA-specific cytolytic T-cell responses of the adaptive immune system are responsible for recovery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: HBV has a direct cytopathic effect on liver cells in all circumstances.
items.reason: Under immunosuppressive conditions post-liver transplantation, HBV may have a direct cytopathic effect.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason is true but does not apply to all circumstances.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Neonatal HBV infection often results in chronic infection.
items.reason: In utero exposure to HBeAg induces T-cell tolerance to nucleocapsid proteins.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains the assertion as T-cell tolerance prevents immunologic clearance, leading to chronic infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.assertion: Adult-acquired HBV infection is associated with a high risk of hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.reason: The host immune response to HBV-infected hepatocytes is typically robust in adults.
items.explanation: While the reason is true, it does not explain the assertion; adult-acquired infection has a low risk of hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.assertion: HCV infection leads to rapid mutation rates.
items.reason: This results in viral quasispecies diversity that evades host immune responses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.assertion: HCV infection is primarily controlled by innate immune responses.
items.reason: NK cells play a crucial role in controlling HCV infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; adaptive immunity plays a significant role, although NK cells are important.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · ■ PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.assertion: Bridging necrosis has significant prognostic value in patients with acute hepatitis.
items.reason: The presence of bridging necrosis was initially associated with a poor prognosis, but this association is no longer upheld.
items.explanation: While bridging necrosis had been thought to have prognostic significance, its demonstration during acute hepatitis is now considered less meaningful.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EXTRAHEPATIC MANIFESTATIONS | PATHOLOGY

items.assertion: Hepatitis C often shows a marked increase in inflammation on histologic examination.
items.reason: The histologic lesion of hepatitis C is notable for a relative paucity of inflammation.
items.explanation: Hepatitis C is characterized by a relative lack of inflammation, not an increase.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EXTRAHEPATIC MANIFESTATIONS | PATHOLOGY

items.assertion: As the frequency of HAV infection declines, the likelihood of clinically apparent illness increases in adults.
items.reason: Hepatitis A tends to be more symptomatic in adults than in children.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains why clinical cases increase as infections decline.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: Hepatitis A vaccination programs have shifted the burden of new infections from children to adults.
items.reason: Vaccination among children has reduced their susceptibility, leaving more susceptible adults at risk.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the shift in infection burden.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: The majority of hepatitis transmitted by blood transfusion is caused by HBV.
items.reason: Most of the hepatitis transmitted by blood transfusion is not caused by HBV.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason correctly states that most hepatitis from blood transfusions is not due to HBV.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.assertion: Intimate contact among toddlers in sub-Saharan Africa contributes significantly to maintaining high frequencies of hepatitis B.
items.reason: Perinatal transmission is the most important mode of HBV perpetuation in East Asia and developing countries.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why intimate contact among toddlers contributes to high hepatitis B frequencies.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)
items.assertion: The prevalence of HDV infection is highest among injection drug users.
items.reason: Injection drug users are frequently exposed to blood and blood products, increasing their risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)
items.assertion: Hepatitis D is rare among persons with chronic hepatitis B in northern Europe.
items.reason: Northern Europe has high rates of HDV infection due to frequent blood exposure.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; HDV infection is confined and not common in northern Europe.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.assertion: The prevalence of HCV infection in the United States was sustained by an aging cohort.
items.reason: This cohort acquired their infections during the 1960s and 1970s, predominantly from self-inoculation with recreational drugs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the prevalence was sustained.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)
items.assertion: The high frequency of HCV infection in Egypt is due to contaminated water supply.
items.reason: Contaminated equipment used for medical procedures and unsafe injection practices contributed to the high prevalence.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; the high frequency was due to medical practices, not water contamination.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.assertion: The incidence of COVID-19 infection is significantly higher than in the previous year.
items.reason: By 2012, HCV mortality had surpassed 100% of the total HIV-infected population.
items.explanation: The statement about COVID-19 incidence is unrelated to HCV mortality surpassing HIV infections.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)
items.assertion: Hepatitis C accounts for the most frequent indication for liver transplantation.
items.reason: Before high-efficacy DAA therapy, hepatitis C was the most common reason for liver transplants.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, with the latter correctly explaining the former.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.assertion: HCV transmission through sexual contact is generally inefficient.
items.reason: Most studies have failed to identify sexual transmission of HCV, and the chances are estimated to be ~5% between monogamous partners.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HCV transmission through sexual contact is inefficient.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)
items.assertion: The prevalence of current HCV infection in the United States decreased from 2010 to 2016.
items.reason: NHANES analysis showed an increase in HCV RNA reactivity from 0.65% to 0.9% during this period.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the prevalence increased, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · TABLE 350-3 Populations with a High Risk for HBV Infection $ ^{a} $ (part 4)

items.assertion: Acute exacerbations of chronic hepatitis B may represent the emergence of a precore mutant.
items.reason: The subsequent course in such patients may be characterized by periodic exacerbations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: The duration of HDV infection is independent of HBV infection.
items.reason: HDV can only occur in the presence of acute or chronic HBV infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the duration of HDV infection depends on the duration of HBV infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: In patients with severe viral hepatitis, bilirubin levels >340 μmol/L are associated with worse prognosis.
items.reason: High serum bilirubin levels extending and persisting late into the course of viral hepatitis indicate more severe disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.assertion: The titer of HBsAg is directly related to the severity of clinical disease in hepatitis B.
items.reason: An inverse correlation exists between serum concentration of HBsAg and degree of liver cell damage.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because there is an inverse correlation, not a direct relationship.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 2)

items.assertion: Testing for HCV RNA can help distinguish between true-positive and false-positive anti-HCV determinations.
items.reason: Nonspecificity in immunoassays for anti-HCV, especially with circulating rheumatoid factor, can lead to false positives.
items.explanation: HCV RNA testing is useful to confirm true-positive results when nonspecificity affects anti-HCV assays.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.assertion: First-generation hybridization assays for HBV DNA are more sensitive than current PCR-based assays.
items.reason: Current PCR assays can detect as few as 10 or 100 virions/mL, whereas first-generation assays had a sensitivity of $10^{5}-10^{6}$ virions/mL.
items.explanation: First-generation hybridization assays are less sensitive than current PCR-based assays.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 3)

items.assertion: Determining HCV RNA level is a reliable marker of disease severity or prognosis.
items.reason: HCV RNA monitoring during and after antiviral therapy helps determine on-treatment and durable responsiveness.
items.explanation: HCV RNA level is not a reliable marker of disease severity or prognosis, although it predicts relative responsiveness to therapy.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.assertion: IgM anti-HBc presence indicates chronic HBV infection.
items.reason: IgM anti-HBc is a reliable indicator of recent infection, not chronic infection.
items.explanation: The presence of IgM anti-HBc indicates recent infection, while its absence suggests an infection in the remote past.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 4)

items.assertion: In patients with established chronic hepatitis B and normal aminotransferase activity, serial testing is often required.
items.reason: This distinguishes between the inactive carrier state and HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis B with fluctuating virologic and necroinflammatory activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why serial testing is necessary.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.assertion: In patients with acute hepatitis-like illness already infected chronically with HBV or HCV, serologic diagnosis can be challenging.
items.reason: This is because the presence of chronic infection markers may obscure the detection of acute viral hepatitis markers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the diagnosis is challenging.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES (part 5)

items.assertion: Hepatitis E can lead to fatal fulminant hepatitis.
items.reason: This complication occurs in up to 20% of cases in pregnant women.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with chronic hepatitis B or C may develop nonliver malignancies.
items.reason: This occurs due to immune complex and other extrahepatic manifestations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 1)

items.assertion: Hepatitis D superinfection can accelerate the progression of chronic hepatitis B.
items.reason: It transforms inactive or mild chronic hepatitis B into severe, progressive disease and increases the risk of cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.assertion: Chronic hepatitis C rarely leads to cirrhosis.
items.reason: Cirrhosis develops in as many as 20% of patients with chronic hepatitis C within 10–20 years.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly states that a significant percentage can develop cirrhosis.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · COMPLICATIONS AND SEQUELAE (part 2)

items.assertion: Acute hepatitis may present with symptoms that are similar to those of acute cholecystitis.
items.reason: Both conditions can cause right upper quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and icterus.
items.explanation: The assertion is true and the reason correctly explains why these conditions may be confused.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: In cases of severe liver injury with aminotransferase levels ≥1000 IU/L, viral hepatitis is the most common cause.
items.reason: Ischemic and drug-induced liver injuries are more commonly associated with such high aminotransferase levels than acute viral hepatitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ischemic and drug-induced liver injuries, especially from acetaminophen, are more common causes of severe liver injury.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Most persons with acute hepatitis A, B, and E recover spontaneously.
items.reason: Specific antiviral therapy is generally not required for these cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.assertion: Forced and prolonged bed rest is essential for full recovery from acute viral hepatitis.
items.reason: Many patients feel better with restricted physical activity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; forced and prolonged bed rest is not essential, though many patients may benefit from restricted physical activity.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 1)

items.assertion: Liver transplantation has become a common and effective treatment for patients with fulminant hepatitis.
items.reason: The cornerstone of management in fulminant hepatitis is meticulous intensive care, not liver transplantation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why liver transplantation is effective.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · Acute Viral Hepatitis (part 2)

items.assertion: For post-exposure prophylaxis, the use of IG has been largely replaced by vaccination.
items.reason: IG is not effective after the incubation period for most common-source outbreaks of hepatitis A.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason provided does not explain why IG has been replaced by vaccination.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Hepatitis A vaccine is recommended for all persons aged ≥12 months for preexposure prophylaxis prior to international travel.
items.reason: The hepatitis A vaccine provides durable protection and simplifies administration compared to IG.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why vaccination is recommended.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Hepatitis A vaccine provides long-lasting protection.
items.reason: Protective levels of anti-HAV should last at least 20 years after vaccination.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.assertion: The first hepatitis B vaccine was derived from recombinant yeast.
items.reason: It consisted of HBsAg particles that were nonglycosylated but otherwise indistinguishable from natural HBsAg.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the first hepatitis B vaccine was prepared from plasma-derived HBsAg particles. The reason is true.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 2)

items.assertion: The incidence of new hepatitis B cases continued to increase in the United States after the introduction of vaccines.
items.reason: Less than 10% of all targeted persons in high-risk groups were actually vaccinated.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why the incidence continued to rise.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.assertion: Heplisav-B is recommended for use in persons aged 18 or older.
items.reason: It has been shown to yield higher proportions with protective levels of anti-HBs compared to Engerix-B.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why Heplisav-B is recommended for persons aged 18 or older.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 3)

items.assertion: Hepatitis D infection can be prevented by vaccinating susceptible persons with the hepatitis B vaccine.
items.reason: There is no product available for immunoprophylaxis to prevent HDV superinfection in chronic HBV patients.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and vaccination with hepatitis B prevents hepatitis D infection.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.assertion: Hepatitis C IG is recommended for postexposure prophylaxis.
items.reason: IG has been shown to be effective in preventing hepatitis C.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; IG is ineffective and not recommended for hepatitis C.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 4)

items.assertion: Immediate reflex treatment in persons who test positive for HCV infection is a part of the ambitious goal toward global eradication by 2030.
items.reason: This approach aims to prevent secondary spread, especially among people who inject drugs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why immediate reflex treatment is part of the goal.
items.reference: Acute Viral Hepatitis · PROPHYLAXIS (part 5)
